parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED at $1 and GREEN at $0.50, with BLUE varying: otter $0.40, peacock $0.50, cat $0.70. Everyone starts with 10 of each chip. Because RED is uniformly the most valuable and GREEN is common-value, the only efficient direction of trade is BLUE flowing toward cat in exchange for RED or, secondarily, GREEN. Otter has the lowest BLUE valuation and is the natural BLUE seller; cat is the high-BLUE buyer; peacock is intermediate and may trade marginally with cat if prices are favorable. However, players only know their own values and the public value ranges, not each other's valuations. They see that BLUE could be anywhere from $0.10 to $1.00 for others, so they face uncertainty about how attractive BLUE is to the others. With simultaneous responses and random partner selection when multiple accept, proposers cannot target a specific partner and must design offers that are personally profitable even if any single counterpart accepts. Normatively, rational behavior is to accept any strictly profitable offer and reject any that lowers one's own total value; the surplus calculator makes this salient. Personas suggest all three prefer structure and are fairly patient, with cat the most prosocial, otter cooperative but cautious, and peacock inequality-tolerant and somewhat pragmatic. That mix makes mutually beneficial BLUE-for-RED trades between otter and cat particularly likely if either stumbles onto a price that makes BLUE cheap for otter and valuable for cat. Over three short rounds, though, there is limited time to learn valuations, so we are likely to see conservative early offers, some declined proposals that reveal hints about others' preferences, and one or two actually executed trades once someone proposes a clearly win–win BLUE-for-RED exchange. Because GREEN is common-value and already priced exactly at its value, it mainly serves as a safe medium of exchange or small-price adjustment, not a big source of surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold symmetric portfolios and have no direct information about others’ BLUE valuations beyond the public range. Otter, a methodical and cooperative type, is likely to open with a modest, easily understandable trade using GREEN to avoid giving up RED immediately. Peacock, who likes structure and is comfortable with some inequality, will likely test an offer that improves her position without pushing extreme terms. Cat, who is prosocial and high in BLUE value, is initially in exploration mode: she knows BLUE is valuable to her but not how much others value it, so she will be cautious about overpaying in RED while still open to favorable BLUE purchases. Declines in this round mostly serve as information signals about what is unattractive, setting up better-calibrated trades in later rounds.

Going into round 1, the only executed trade so far is peacock selling 2 BLUE to cat for 1 RED, revealing that at least one counterpart (cat) is willing to trade RED for BLUE at a relatively RED-favorable rate. Otter has seen that BLUE can buy RED from someone, which makes her low BLUE valuation salient: she can profit by offloading BLUE for RED if she finds a partner. Cat has signaled a strong appetite for BLUE by accepting peacock’s offer; this will incline her to keep seeking BLUE-for-RED trades, but she must avoid overpaying. Peacock, having completed a mildly profitable trade, feels that 2 BLUE for 1 RED is an acceptable benchmark and may probe around that rate while staying conservative. This round should see more explicitly surplus-positive BLUE-for-RED proposals as players adjust to the revealed willingness to trade.

By round 2, two BLUE-for-RED trades have occurred: peacock→cat at 2 BLUE for 1 RED and cat→otter at 3 RED for 2 BLUE. These outcomes tell everyone that (i) cat values BLUE a lot, (ii) otter is willing to accept BLUE when heavily compensated in RED, and (iii) peacock can profitably shed BLUE for RED but faces competitive pressure from cat’s aggressive bids. Holdings are now asymmetric, and each participant has only one remaining proposal opportunity. With little time left, risk-averse and structure-seeking tendencies push them toward clean, clearly beneficial trades rather than complex experiments. Peacock is best positioned to propose another straightforward BLUE-for-RED exchange to cat; otter, after gaining RED, becomes more cautious about giving it up again unless strongly advantaged; and cat is still eager to convert some RED into additional BLUE if she can secure terms that leave her better off. Final proposals will likely be sharper—closer to each player’s true indifference curves—but some otherwise-efficient trades may fail if the remaining players’ reservation prices no longer overlap after earlier deals.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips alongside common-value green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color and wants to maximize their own monetary value, using an on-screen calculator that reveals the exact dollar gain or loss for any contemplated trade from their own perspective. Because valuations differ for red and blue, mutually beneficial trades exist, especially between players whose values are highly misaligned. Here, lion highly values blue ($1.00) and somewhat values red ($0.40); iguana highly values red ($0.80) and modestly values blue ($0.40); alligator sits in the middle (blue $0.60, red $0.30). Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so green acts as a neutral currency that can help compose balanced deals but does not create pure-surplus arbitrage on its own.

The dominant efficiency patterns are: (1) move blue chips toward lion; (2) move red chips toward iguana; and (3) use green or opposite-color chips to compensate those who give up their valuable color. For example, iguana gains from giving away blue for red or green; lion gains from giving away red or green for blue; alligator can profit by intermediating trades if she exchanges away relatively low-valued colors for higher-valued ones. However, players do not know others’ valuations, so they must infer who values what from observed behavior. Proposals that look generous or one-sided may signal strong preferences; rejections reveal which trades are not attractive at the responder’s values.

Given the personas, alligator is reflective, stability-oriented, and somewhat cautious about exploitation, likely to start with conservative, modestly profitable offers and to avoid trades that show a clear loss on her calculator. Lion is patient, logically strong, and relatively tolerant of unequal splits, so she may propose or accept trades that create clear surplus for her even if they are lopsided for others and may experiment more to explore others’ values. Iguana is rational, inequality-tolerant, and moderately cautious, likely to accept clearly profitable offers and decline losses without strong fairness-driven resistance. Across the three rounds, we should expect: early tentative, information-gathering proposals; mid-game adjustments once players infer some patterns (e.g., that lion pursues blue, iguana pursues red); and late-game attempts to lock in high-value color concentrations before time runs out. Random selection among multiple acceptors adds risk: senders cannot target a specific partner, so they may design offers that are acceptable for more than one counterpart or slant offers so only the intended type is likely to accept. Overall, we forecast incremental, self-interested bargaining with some exploratory offers, mostly avoiding trades that show a net loss to any accepting responder, and a gradual funneling of chips toward the players who value them most.

Note: All numeric surplus references in explanations are approximate, based strictly on the given valuations for this hypothetical forecast.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and have no information about others’ private values. Proposals in this round are likely to be exploratory yet self-protective, using small quantities that show a clear personal gain on the proposer’s calculator. Alligator, as a reflective and exploitation-cautious player, will likely test a modest trade that increases her value without risking a big loss. Lion, highly valuing blue, is incentivized to start shifting her red or green into blue but must do so without knowing that others value blue less. Iguana, highly valuing red, will be inclined to shift blue away in exchange for red or green if she spots profitable terms. Because everyone is value-uncertain about others, early rejections and acceptances are informative: rejections to clearly favorable offers (for the sender) could indicate the color is important to responders; easy acceptances reveal low valuations for the chip being traded away.

By round 1, players have seen some informative behavior. Alligator’s first trade asked for green in exchange for red, signaling she may value green at least as much as red, but both others rejected, suggesting they are not eager to part with green cheaply. Lion’s accepted trade where she gave up three red for two blue (which was profitable for both lion and iguana) reveals that lion places a high value on blue relative to red, while iguana is quite willing to trade away blue for red. Iguana’s failed attempt to give up blue for green, with both others declining, suggests that neither alligator nor lion wants to pay that particular price to acquire more green, further underscoring that green is treated as a neutral or slightly tight resource early on. In this context, round 1 is likely to feature more targeted red–blue swaps, with lion and iguana both trying to accumulate their favored colors and alligator cautiously searching for risk-free improvements or intermediary opportunities.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer. Lion has consistently tried to acquire blue and is willing to part with red and even green; iguana has willingly exchanged blue and green for red when profitable; alligator has made modest, calculator-positive moves that edge her toward more blue at the cost of low-valued red. The chip distribution has already shifted somewhat: lion holds fewer red and green and more blue; iguana holds more red and fewer blue/green; alligator leans slightly toward blue as well. With no future rounds, players have no reason to preserve options; instead, they aim to lock in any remaining gains that their calculators show as clearly profitable, even if small. This encourages relatively safe, incremental proposals over large, risky swaps. We forecast lion and iguana making final attempts to fine-tune their red–blue holdings, while alligator remains conservative—willing to accept free gains but unlikely to post very aggressive offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This 3-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values to each player, while green chips are common-value and thus strategically neutral except as a way to smooth trades. Each player starts with 10 of each color and acts three times as proposer, once per round, in a fixed order. Because each player privately values chips differently, there are clear surplus-creating opportunities: for example, both whale-red-86 and duck-orange-4971 value RED at $1.00, while dolphin-orange-3122 values RED at $0.60; conversely, dolphin values BLUE at $0.80, higher than whale at $0.40 and duck at $0.60. That means efficient gains come from transferring RED from dolphin to whale or duck, and BLUE from whale to dolphin. GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone and gives no surplus by itself, but can be used like cash or a tie-breaker in multi-round bargaining.

The players’ personas suggest generally cooperative, inequality-tolerant behavior with moderate caution against exploitation. None has particularly strong norm-enforcement, so they are unlikely to reject small, positive but unequal gains just to punish others. They also see a calculator that tells them exactly their own gain or loss for each proposal, so they should never accept strictly negative expected-value trades. However, because they do not know others’ values, they must infer them from observed proposals and acceptances. Early in the game, they will test the waters with modest, positive-surplus offers and adjust based on who accepts. Over only three rounds, there is limited time for complex reputation strategies, so they will focus on obviously beneficial trades rather than multi-step setups.

In this forecast, whale-red-86, as a highly cooperative and relatively numerate player who values RED the most and BLUE the least, will often attempt to trade BLUE away to dolphin-orange-3122 in exchange for RED, since that is the clearest surplus opportunity. Dolphin, who values BLUE highly and RED relatively little, is a natural counterpart and will tend to accept any trade that increases his dollar value even if it appears somewhat unequal. Duck-orange-4971, with high RED and moderate BLUE values and very low norm-enforcement, is willing to accept modest gains, and is also a good target for selling BLUE or GREEN in exchange for RED.

A key uncertainty is competition for favorable offers: when a proposer makes a profitable trade for more than one counterpart, multiple players may accept, forcing a random draw that the proposer cannot control. This risk will make proposers slightly conservative in sizing offers: they may try to gain value even if only one of the possible counterparts has strong incentives, but keep trades simple enough that exploiting common-value GREEN doesn’t cause unnecessary competition. Across three rounds, we expect: (1) early feeling-out proposals where others may decline if surplus is too small; (2) mid-game convergence on clearer cross-valuation opportunities (RED from dolphin to whale/duck, BLUE from whale to dolphin/duck); and (3) late-game attempts by each player to grab a final, possibly slightly aggressive gain given that there is no continuation payoff beyond round 2.

The reconstructed play below respects the private incentives given the valuations and personas: all accepted trades are strictly profitable to both sides given their own values, declined trades are unprofitable or too small to be attractive under mild caution, and the pattern of who trades with whom is consistent with the obvious comparative advantage in RED and BLUE values.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins with symmetric endowments and complete uncertainty about others’ private values. Players will use this round to probe: whale-red-86 is likely to test trading away low-value BLUE or GREEN for higher-value RED; dolphin-orange-3122 will explore whether someone is willing to give him BLUE in exchange for his lower-valued RED or GREEN; and duck-orange-4971 will be open to modest gains but not eager to initiate complex trades. Because nobody yet knows who values which colors, we expect an initial declined offer or two as proposals misalign with counterparts’ incentives, followed by at least one clearly profitable, accepted trade that reveals some structure of values.

By round 1, the accepted dolphin–whale trade in round 0 has partially revealed that dolphin is willing to part with RED for BLUE and that whale values such trades. Whale has increased RED and decreased BLUE; dolphin has the opposite shift. Duck still has her initial holdings but has learned from observing that BLUE is attractive to dolphin and that whale is actively seeking RED. Players now refine their proposals: whale should push further into converting BLUE (and possibly GREEN) into RED; dolphin may try again to acquire BLUE while offloading RED; duck will experiment with small trades that offload her lower-valued BLUE to either dolphin or whale in exchange for RED, but may misprice her initial attempt given the limited information about others’ valuations.

Round 2 is the final opportunity for gains. At this point, everyone has seen repeated patterns: dolphin consistently trades away RED for BLUE or GREEN, signaling his relatively low valuation of RED and high valuation of BLUE; whale has been a steady buyer of RED, sometimes paying with BLUE or GREEN; duck has now transacted once with dolphin and understands that she can profitably swap some RED for BLUE as well. With no future rounds, players no longer worry about future bargaining power and simply look for any remaining positive-surplus trades. Whale, now quite RED-heavy and BLUE-poor, has limited remaining flexibility; dolphin seeks to fine-tune his portfolio with small, sure gains; duck looks for one last easy improvement. Proposals that do not create clear, strictly positive gains will be declined, while modest, obviously beneficial one-step trades are likely to be accepted without much concern for equality.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading two privately valued chip colors (RED and BLUE) plus a common-value GREEN chip. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, but RED and BLUE differ by player, creating gains-from-trade opportunities when someone values a chip more than the current holder. TURTLE values BLUE the most ($1.00) and RED slightly less ($0.90). GORILLA values RED the most ($1.00) and BLUE at $0.70. ZEBRA is lowest on both private colors (RED $0.60, BLUE $0.50) and so is in the best position to profitably sell both RED and BLUE to the higher valuers. All three are at least moderately prosocial and communicative, with ZEBRA especially generosity-forward and GORILLA cooperation-oriented, but everyone’s calculator shows their own exact gain or loss from any offer, so choices will still be driven mainly by private surplus. TURTLE’s strong reasoning and medium exploitation caution make her likely to push for clearly profitable exchanges while trying to avoid being underpaid. GORILLA’s stability and lower norm-enforcement suggest she might accept small but positive gains without aggressive bargaining. ZEBRA is highly generous and tolerant of unequal outcomes, so she is especially likely to accept modest positive trades that give others larger gains, as long as she is not losing money. A key uncertainty is that no one knows the others’ values, so early offers serve as signals: for example, TURTLE asking for BLUE suggests she values BLUE highly. Players must also account for random partner selection when multiple players accept: a proposer cannot choose which accepter they trade with, which pushes them toward neutral or slightly conservative pricing so that any accepting counterparty is still acceptable. Over three short rounds with everyone proposing each round, we are likely to see: (1) exploratory offers early that test whether others value specific colors, (2) moves to exploit obvious comparative advantages (TURTLE and GORILLA trying to buy their favorite color from ZEBRA), and (3) some missed gains because proposals must be simple one-color-for-one-color trades and must attract at least one strictly positive counterpart.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but have asymmetric values. No one knows others’ exact values, so this round will mainly be exploratory. TURTLE, as a strong reasoner with high BLUE value, is inclined to test the market for BLUE and possibly RED, gauging who is willing to part with which colors. GORILLA, with her highest value on RED, wants to acquire RED cheaply relative to her $1.00 valuation, likely experimenting with offers that give up BLUE or GREEN. ZEBRA, with lower valuations on both private colors, is intrinsically well-positioned as a supplier of both RED and BLUE, and her generosity-forward profile makes her more willing to accept modest gains. However, all three must navigate uncertainty about how others react and about the random partner rule if both potential recipients accept an offer.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. TURTLE has shown she is willing to pay GREEN to acquire BLUE, which hints to others that she values BLUE more than GREEN’s common $0.50, but the exact premium is still unknown. ZEBRA’s acceptance of TURTLE’s BLUE-for-GREEN trade and GORILLA’s RED-for-BLUE trade signals she is relatively flexible and comfortable parting with both BLUE and RED when she sees a small gain. GORILLA’s willingness to trade one-for-one BLUE for RED suggests she values RED at least slightly more than BLUE and is comfortable making moderate but not aggressive demands. Inventory positions have also shifted: TURTLE holds a bit more BLUE and less GREEN; GORILLA holds more RED and less BLUE; ZEBRA holds more BLUE and less RED and GREEN. This makes further profitable trades still possible but changes who can supply which color cheaply. Players now tailor offers more sharply: TURTLE is incentivized to try obtaining RED (which is almost as valuable to her as BLUE) from the now-RED-rich GORILLA or from ZEBRA if she still has RED; GORILLA wants additional RED if available at good terms, while ZEBRA may seek to rebuild GREEN or to offload her relatively low-value private colors when it yields clear gains.

Heading into the final round, players have a clearer sense of relative tastes: TURTLE appears eager for BLUE and somewhat for RED; GORILLA has visibly accumulated RED and swapped BLUE for RED and for GREEN; ZEBRA has repeatedly sold private-color chips when she could earn modest gains, often in ways that helped others more. The game ends after this round, so there is no reason to shade offers for future reputation; each proposal should maximize immediate expected value. Each proposer must now weigh: (1) remaining inventories after prior trades, (2) which counterparties likely still value specific colors highly, and (3) the random selection among multiple acceptors, which discourages very asymmetric offers that would be terrible if the “wrong” partner accepts. TURTLE will likely attempt one more value-improving swap that leans into her strong BLUE preference, but must contend with being somewhat BLUE-rich already. GORILLA, with many RED chips, might consider converting surplus RED or remaining BLUE into GREEN or a slightly more balanced portfolio if it yields a small gain. ZEBRA, as a generous type and likely holder of relatively low-value private chips, may make a final attempt at a mutually beneficial trade but is not strongly driven to squeeze every cent, and if reasonable offers do not produce gains for at least one counterpart, they will rationally decline.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player, while green chips are common-value and therefore strategically neutral aside from inventory constraints. Each player starts with 10 of each chip type and aims to maximize their own monetary payoff based on their private per-chip values. The main gains from trade arise where one player's low-value chip is another player's high-value chip.

Private values:
- cat-green-4185 (cat): RED $0.90, BLUE $0.60, GREEN $0.50
- iguana-yellow-3262 (iguana): RED $0.80, BLUE $0.30, GREEN $0.50
- zebra-pink-2457 (zebra): RED $0.40, BLUE $1.00, GREEN $0.50

Thus, cat and iguana both value red relatively highly and blue less; zebra values blue very highly and red relatively low. This creates strong natural trading opportunities: zebra is an efficient buyer of blue and seller of red, whereas cat and iguana are efficient buyers of red and sellers of blue. Green chips are worth the same to everyone, so trades involving green have no intrinsic surplus and are mainly useful as perceived signals or mistakes; rational, calculator-using players will quickly see that green-for-anything trades are zero- or negative-surplus for at least one side and therefore unappealing.

Key dynamics and incentives:
1. Surplus creation: The biggest potential surplus is in trades where zebra gives up red (low for zebra, high for cat/iguana) in exchange for blue (high for zebra, lower for cat/iguana). Mutually beneficial exchange rates exist in wide bands (e.g., zebra might trade 1 RED for 1 BLUE and still benefit, while the red buyer also benefits). Because each player has a surplus calculator, they can identify such win–win trades easily from their own perspective, but they must infer whether the counterpart also sees value.

2. Incomplete information: Each player knows only their own valuation structure. From the standpoint of beliefs, they know all chips lie within known public ranges (red and blue between $0.10 and $1.00, green fixed at $0.50), but they do not know exactly how others rank red versus blue. Players must infer others' values from observed offers and acceptances. For example, if zebra eagerly accepts losing red for blue, others will infer zebra values blue more and red less.

3. Random partner assignment among accepters: When multiple players can profit from a proposal, they may all accept, but the actual partner is then chosen at random among accepters. This creates a strategic externality: a proposer targeting, say, zebra as the ideal partner might unintentionally trade with iguana instead if both accept. Rational proposers will thus often design offers that are attractive to the intended counterpart but not to the third player, using chip color choice and quantity to tilt incentives.

4. Proposal incentives and personas:
- cat is a strong logical reasoner, relatively tolerant of inequality, and fairly flexible; he is likely to focus quickly on positive expected-value trades and not get stuck on equal splits. As the first proposer each round, cat will experiment early with cross-color trades and adjust based on acceptances.
- iguana is reflective, structure-seeking, and highly averse to being exploited. She is likely to avoid any trade that shows a loss or even very small gain on her calculator, preferring clear, stable improvements. She may be conservative in accepting proposals that look even slightly unbalanced.
- zebra is cooperation-oriented and patient, with moderate exploitation caution. She is willing to accept clearly beneficial trades, even if they create unequal gains, so long as she is not visibly losing. Being the player with the strongest blue value, she stands to gain from accumulating blue at the cost of red, and may be the most active acceptor of such offers.

5. Temporal dynamics across the three rounds: Because there are only three rounds with each player proposing once per round (nine proposal opportunities total), there is limited time for elaborate signaling. Early proposals are likely to be exploratory but still value-positive for the proposer; rejected offers quickly teach players about others' likely valuations (e.g., if no one accepts a generous-looking red-for-blue trade from cat, he might infer others also like red). As the game proceeds, players should converge on red–blue swaps that match underlying comparative advantages.

6. Strategic uncertainty: Each player sees only their own surplus calculator and the accept/decline patterns. They must infer others' motives and valuations, and might worry about adverse selection (e.g., worrying that someone accepting a trade might know something they don't). However, because there is no hidden state beyond values and everyone sees direct net payoff for themselves on each proposed trade, many trades that are clearly positive from both sides' perspectives will still go through.

Putting this together, the most likely path in this game is that some early proposals probe willingness to trade across red/blue at simple 1:1 or 2:1 ratios. Zebra will try to convert red into blue, and cat and iguana will try to convert blue into red. Rejections will mostly prune out trades that sacrifice too much surplus or mistakenly involve green. Over three rounds, we expect some successful high-surplus red–blue trades, especially between zebra and one of the red-valuing players, but not complete exploitation of all possible gains due to uncertainty, random partner assignment, and cautious behavior by iguana.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the exploratory phase. No one has yet seen how others value red vs. blue, so proposals will be relatively simple and conservative. Cat moves first and is likely to test others' willingness to exchange red for blue at a near-even rate that is favorable but not extreme from his perspective, trying to sell his weaker blue or buy more valuable red. Iguana, being structure-seeking and exploitation-cautious, will likely follow with a clearly positive red–blue trade where she sells blue to buy red, but at a conservative ratio that ensures she benefits even if others also value red highly. Zebra, realizing she benefits from holding blue and giving up red, will also probe with a trade that exchanges red for blue at a favorable but not outrageous rate. Early acceptances will clarify who is the best counterpart for red–blue trades, with iguana especially wary of small gains and zebra relatively open to cooperation.

By Round 1, players have seen that zebra is willing to exchange some blue for red at parity (she accepted cat’s 2 RED for 2 BLUE), and that neither cat nor zebra was willing to give up red for blue at parity (both declined iguana’s 2 RED for 2 BLUE and zebra’s opposite-direction offer). This reveals that red is generally more prized than blue across the group, but especially by cat and iguana. Cat now knows that zebra values blue more than red relative to himself, and will adjust by proposing a trade where zebra sells red and buys blue. Iguana, having seen little success and being loss-averse, will probe a more favorable-for-her ratio, trying to sell more blue in exchange for red, while still seeking a clean, safe gain. Zebra, seeing her best counterpart is someone who values red strongly, will try once more to offer red in exchange for blue but at a ratio that is slightly less favorable to the counterparty, counting on cat’s or iguana’s desire for red to overcome the imbalance.

Entering Round 2, the pattern is clear: cat and iguana both strongly prefer red over blue and are willing to give up substantial amounts of blue for red when it yields clear calculator gains. Zebra, for her part, has been the primary buyer of blue and seller of red, but her last attempt to trade 4 RED for 3 BLUE failed, indicating that cat and iguana now resist any trade that does not show them a sizeable net gain. With only one proposal each remaining, players will aim for straightforward, clearly positive-sum trades. Cat will design an offer that gives zebra red for blue at a modest but still positive margin for him, trusting that zebra will recognize the mutual benefit. Iguana, having successfully traded with zebra once, will mirror this behavior but keep her risk low by offering a ratio that is generous enough to be accepted but still positive on her calculator. Zebra, recognizing that further red-for-blue offers at stingy ratios are unlikely to be accepted, may make a final, more conciliatory proposal that offers red at a rate attractive to either cat or iguana, hoping to close one more profitable trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players begin with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values on red and blue chips. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green serves as a transparent benchmark and a natural medium of exchange. Monkey and Camel both value blue chips highly (0.90 and 0.80) and red at 0.20 and 0.50 respectively, while Gorilla values both red and blue relatively low at 0.30 each. This structure creates strong mutual-gain opportunities: Gorilla is an efficient seller of both red and blue, especially to Monkey (who loves blue) and somewhat to Camel (who likes both red and blue more than Gorilla does). All players strictly prefer gaining value over equalizing holdings, and the UI calculator makes it salient when a trade is profitable in private terms.

Because only one chip color can be bought and another sold in each proposal, trades are simple bilateral exchanges that may be accepted by either of the two potential partners; if both accept, the partner is randomly selected, creating uncertainty for proposers who might want to target a specific counterpart. Proposers must therefore set terms that are personally profitable while still attractive to at least one responder, often leaving some surplus to the counterparty. Gorilla, patient and stability-oriented, is likely to avoid risk of rejection by proposing modestly win–win trades, particularly selling red or blue in exchange for green or blue she values equally. Camel, a strong reasoner and resource-protective, will seek clear positive value and is willing to tolerate unequal outcomes; he will avoid any trade that looks exploitative against him, but not insist on fairness for others. Monkey is more prosocial and may accept modestly positive deals that create clear mutual gain, even if he could theoretically bargain for slightly better.

Over three rounds (nine total proposals), players must balance immediate gain from sure profitable trades against the hope of better future offers. However, given the short horizon and lack of rich communication, they are unlikely to hold out aggressively. The most likely dynamic is that a few clearly mutually beneficial trades occur early (especially exchanging Gorilla’s low-value blue/red for others’ green or blue), gradually polarizing holdings toward each player’s comparative advantage: Monkey and Camel accumulate more blue, Camel may pick up extra red, and Gorilla accumulates more green (and possibly sheds some blue/red). There will also be some declined proposals when terms are either too generous to the proposer or mistakenly misaligned with counterpart valuations (unknown to the proposer). The game ends with a pattern where Monkey has substantially more blue, Camel has a mixed surplus in red and blue, and Gorilla has more green and somewhat reduced red/blue, reflecting exploitation of gains from comparative advantage without explicit collusion.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, no one has observed behavior yet, so all proposals reflect only private valuations and very general expectations about others. Gorilla, valuing both red and blue at 0.30 and green at 0.50, is naturally inclined to trade away red or blue for green. Camel and Monkey each know only their own valuation pattern but not each other's, so they may tentatively probe with offers that are privately appealing and plausibly acceptable. Because the horizon is short and risk of mutual rejection is salient, first-round trades that are clearly positive for at least one responder are likely to be accepted quickly.

By the start of round 1, everyone has seen that Gorilla was willing to sell red for green at fairly generous terms, and that Monkey eagerly bought blue from Gorilla using green. This reveals Gorilla’s relative eagerness to turn red/blue into green and suggests to Camel and Monkey that Gorilla undervalues red/blue compared with them. Camel also learned that his initial attempt to trade red for blue at 3-for-2 was unattractive to both partners, signaling that others likely value blue more or red less than he does. Monkey, having profited from the first round, is now more confident that offering green for blue from Gorilla can continue to generate value. As a result, second-round proposals are more targeted: Gorilla leans further into selling red or blue for green, Camel adjusts to slightly sweeter terms when trying to get blue, and Monkey presses his advantage in accumulating blue while remaining mildly prosocial.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer. Gorilla has repeatedly signaled willingness to sacrifice value to convert red/blue into green, while Monkey has accumulated blue through profitable trades. Camel has not yet completed a successful trade and has seen that his earlier blue-acquisition attempts were rejected, so he becomes more conservative, focusing only on trades that are clearly positive. With no future rounds to consider, everyone is myopically focused on immediate surplus: they will accept any strictly profitable trade and reject zero or negative ones. Proposers try one last time to exploit comparative advantages, but at this point holdings and previous moves limit the remaining mutually beneficial deals, so a mix of one more accepted trade and a couple of declined attempts is likely.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip counts (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it functions like a stable currency and safe reserve; none of them has a strict incentive to trade green away unless they get clear value in return or are probing others’ preferences. Alligator values red the most ($0.70) and blue the least ($0.30). Otter is the mirror image on red/blue (blue=$0.50, red=$0.30). Tiger is intermediate (red=$0.60, blue=$0.20). This creates clear gains-from-trade on red ↔ blue between alligator and otter (and somewhat with tiger), but each player only knows their own valuations. They must infer others’ values from which trades are proposed and accepted. Because each proposal can only involve one color offered and one color requested, and acceptance decisions are simultaneous and private, there is both strategic pricing and partner-competition: a proposer who constructs a lucrative-for-them, still-nonnegative offer might induce multiple acceptances and then a random partner, so they cannot fully target a specific counterpart. Personas suggest they are generally thoughtful, modestly prosocial but not strict fairness enforcers. Alligator is logical, patient, generous, and relatively tolerant of unequal outcomes, so likely to propose trades with a decent surplus for himself that are still attractive to at least one partner, and to accept positive but not tiny gains. Otter is reflective, inequality-tolerant, and risk/uncertainty-averse; she is unlikely to accept losses, but will accept modest gains if the structure feels clear. Tiger is cooperative, short-horizon (less focused on future negotiating leverage), and behaviorally stable, so he is willing to take small sure gains now and will quickly learn to pounce on obviously favorable deals. Over three rounds, we therefore expect early cautious, information-revealing offers (especially by alligator and otter) and relatively quick convergence on red↔blue swaps that exploit known comparative advantages once at least one party learns something from accept/decline patterns. A key uncertainty is that each player is unsure who values which chip more; they use observed acceptances to infer relative valuations and then adjust their proposals in later rounds to be just generous enough to elicit acceptance without giving away too much surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, holdings are symmetric and no one knows others’ red/blue values. Rational structure suggests red↔blue trades are promising, with alligator likely a red seller and blue buyer, and otter the opposite. Tiger, with mid-range values, can profitably trade with either side. In this first round, each proposer is likely to test simple, small-size trades to gauge who bites. Given their personas, they will avoid obvious losses but may accept small positive gains even if the proposer appears to benefit more, as they are not strong fairness enforcers and they want to avoid overthinking in a short game.

After round 0, some structure has become clearer. Alligator and otter have effectively revealed opposite preferences over red and blue via the first trade, and otter’s sizeable blue-for-red swap with tiger shows tiger’s willingness to grab obviously profitable blue-for-red opportunities. Both otter and tiger now hold more red and less blue than at the start, while alligator holds fewer red and more blue. Still, none of them knows exact values, only that counterparties seem happy to trade along this dimension. In round 1, proposers can refine their offers—tilting quantities so that they gain more surplus while still leaving clear nonnegative gains for at least one counterpart. Tiger, having just benefited from otter’s generous exchange, may now become more selective, but his short horizon suggests he will still accept clearly positive deals rather than hold out for perfect ones.

By the final round, each player has seen multiple red↔blue offers and can infer that red is generally more prized than blue by others, though they still do not know exact valuations. Alligator, as a strong logical reasoner, focuses on proposals that slightly improve his already favorable red-heavy portfolio while avoiding overpaying in blue or green. Otter has learned that she can often gain by trading her red for blue when the rate is favorable, but may now be cautious about worsening her position too much in a final move. Tiger has already locked in good gains and, being short-horizon but reflective, will still accept clearly positive offers but may refuse marginal ones that seem to give too much away. With only three turns left, proposals are more “take-it-or-leave-it,” and each player is more willing to let a deal go if it is not clearly beneficial on their calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values over two negotiable chip types (RED, BLUE) plus a common-value GREEN chip that is worth $0.50 to everyone. All start with 10 of each color. Dolphin values BLUE the most and RED the least; Frog values RED the most and BLUE the least; Pig values both RED and BLUE moderately. This creates strong cross-player gains from trade, especially swapping Dolphin’s low-value RED for Frog’s low-value BLUE, and having Pig as an intermediate trader when direct swaps are unattractive.

Key incentives: Dolphin (BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.20, GREEN=$0.50) wants to convert RED into BLUE or, second-best, into GREEN; will be reluctant to give up BLUE and fairly neutral on GREEN vs. BLUE because they are close in value. Frog (RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50) wants to dump BLUE and acquire RED; is somewhat indifferent between BLUE and GREEN (0.30 vs. 0.50) but strongly prefers RED. Pig (RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.40, GREEN=$0.50) is nearly symmetric on RED/BLUE and slightly prefers GREEN; Pig can serve as a flexible middleman because they are often close to indifferent in trades that are valuable for the others.

Strategically, nobody observes others’ exact valuations, only the common range. Each player, however, sees the exact dollar impact of any proposed trade on their own payoff via the surplus calculator. This makes accept/decline decisions locally rational: players will almost never accept trades that are negative for them. But proposals must be constructed under valuation uncertainty: proposers don’t know how much the others value each color and must infer it from general ranges and responses over time.

Given the personas, all three are cooperation-oriented, with Dolphin and Frog especially prosocial and structure-seeking, and Pig somewhat more intuitive and short-term. We should expect relatively fair, non-exploitative offers with visible surplus for the responder, along with some experimentation in early rounds to learn what others might accept. Dolphin is likely to start with structured, modest-win propositions; Frog may aim for balanced trades that clearly benefit others; Pig may take simpler, more intuitive shots at seemingly symmetric swaps. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), we expect a few mutually beneficial trades to execute, gradually reallocating chips so Dolphin ends with more BLUE and fewer RED, Frog with more RED and fewer BLUE, and Pig opportunistically improving GREEN holdings or taking side-gain from intermediating exchanges.

Because acceptance is simultaneous and random among multiple acceptors, proposers can’t hand-pick a partner; this makes them shy away from extreme sweetheart deals targeted at one player. Instead, they will often propose terms that would be weakly or moderately good for either potential recipient. Uncertainty about how much surplus others require means some early proposals will be declined; accepted trades will tend to be those where proposers leave clear room for counterpart profit while still improving their own portfolios.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has yet observed others’ behavior, so beliefs about valuations are diffuse: they only know that RED and BLUE values lie between $0.10 and $1.00 for everyone, and GREEN is common-value at $0.50. Round 0 is mainly exploratory: players test simple, easily interpretable trades that seem likely to be mutually beneficial. Dolphin will likely probe swaps that move her away from low-value RED toward BLUE or GREEN; Frog will probe exchanging BLUE for RED in a way that still looks good to the responder; Pig will test balanced-looking trades that marginally favor himself while giving clear upside to one counterpart.

After Round 0, trade history reveals partial information. Dolphin has signaled she is willing to give up multiple RED for a modest number of BLUE, suggesting RED is relatively cheap and BLUE valuable to her. Frog has shown willingness to give up 3 BLUE for only 2 RED, indicating that RED is especially valuable and BLUE relatively weak. Pig has accepted both earlier trades, implying that, from his perspective, they were profitable, which in turn suggests his valuations for RED and BLUE are moderate. Pig’s declined attempt to buy GREEN with BLUE hints that at least one counterpart values GREEN at or above Pig’s ask. In Round 1, players refine strategies: Dolphin may now explicitly target Frog’s surplus in RED; Frog will likely push further on BLUE-for-RED trades while keeping them attractive to others; Pig will be more cautious in asking for GREEN and may instead leverage his flexible RED/BLUE holdings.

By Round 2, the pattern is clearer. Dolphin consistently trades away RED, signaling it is her weakest chip and that she values BLUE and GREEN more. Frog’s behavior has emphasized accumulating RED while being willing to part with BLUE and, to a lesser extent, GREEN, indicating her top priority is RED. Pig has repeatedly taken trades that marginally increase his overall value, often acting as a middleman between Dolphin and Frog’s more polarized preferences, but has learned that counterparts are reluctant to give up GREEN except at favorable rates. With only three proposal turns left, each player weighs whether incremental improvements are worth risking proposals that will be declined. Dolphin and Frog, both structure-seeking and prosocial, are likely to propose relatively conservative, clearly win-win trades. Pig, already having captured some surplus, may either try one last modest GREEN-improving trade or settle for a simple RED-BLUE balance adjustment that appears safe to at least one partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. The private values for RED and BLUE create strong gains from trade: iguana values BLUE highly ($0.80) and RED weakly ($0.20); owl values RED highly ($0.70) and BLUE weakly ($0.30); squid is intermediate (BLUE $0.50, RED $0.10). Everyone only sees their own values and the public chip holdings and trade history. This creates uncertainty about exactly who likes which color, but the range information (RED/BLUE between $0.10 and $1.00) and the existence of a surplus calculator allow each player to focus on proposals that are privately profitable. Because any nonzero-profit trade is visible as a gain on the calculator, players have a strong incentive to reject negative-surplus offers and accept clearly positive-surplus trades. The main bargaining tension is about the division of surplus, not about whether a trade is good in absolute terms. Iguana is a logical, exploitation-cautious planner who will avoid any deal that looks even slightly negative. Owl is intuitive and inequality-tolerant, likely to accept modest but clear gains. Squid is cooperative and empathic but also structure-seeking, likely to look for clean, understandable deals that visibly increase her surplus. With three rounds and three proposals per round, proposers must weigh how attractive to make their offers: if they demand too aggressive a price, both potential recipients will simply decline; if they are more generous, at least one recipient is likely to accept, but random selection between multiple accepters can introduce risk. Over time, observed trades and rejections reveal partial information about others’ preferences (e.g., who consistently buys RED or BLUE), enabling somewhat better-targeted proposals in later rounds. However, with only three rounds, there is limited time for full unraveling of private values, so players will likely rely on simple, one-step profitable trades that clearly improve their own payoffs while leaving enough on the table that at least one counterpart’s calculator shows a gain too.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet revealed their color preferences through trades, so proposals are exploratory. Iguana, as a careful logical reasoner with high exploitation caution, is likely to start with a conservative, clearly self-profitable offer that tests whether anyone strongly values the color he is selling. Owl and squid will respond based purely on their calculators: they will accept only if their own net value is positive and large enough to feel worthwhile. Because everyone is still uncertain about others’ valuations, initial proposals may be somewhat misaligned with counterpart incentives and more likely to be declined, with later turns in the round starting to react to any accepted deals or visible patterns.

By the start of round 1, the history shows that squid was willing to sell RED for BLUE (suggesting she values BLUE at least somewhat) and that iguana was willing to buy BLUE for GREEN. Iguana also just accepted BLUE for GREEN, revealing that he values BLUE more than GREEN. Owl has bought RED with BLUE, suggesting a relatively high value on RED and lower on BLUE. Even though players lack exact numbers, these patterns give coarse information: iguana likely wants BLUE, owl wants RED, and squid wants both BLUE and perhaps still some RED if the price is right. Proposers will now try to exploit these revealed tendencies, offering trades that are more tailored (e.g., giving up low-value colors for them in exchange for high-value colors) while still keeping enough surplus on the table for at least one counterpart to see a positive gain. Small-quantity trades become attractive to control risk if both potential counterparts might accept.

In the final round, players know this is their last chance to improve payoffs. Iguana has repeatedly bought BLUE and sold RED or GREEN, confirming his strong BLUE preference and low RED value. Owl has repeatedly bought RED with BLUE and later with RED/BLUE mix, signaling high RED value. Squid has shown a willingness to sell RED and some GREEN for BLUE and to sell RED for BLUE again, revealing that BLUE is her relatively most valued of the two private colors, but she also avoids steep losses. With the endgame approaching, proposers become slightly more willing to propose generous, obviously profitable trades for counterparts rather than holding out for perfect prices, since unexploited surplus will simply be lost. Risk of overpaying is still present (especially for iguana with his high exploitation caution), but each proposer balances that against the fact that there will be no future rounds to make better deals or learn more. Small, clean exchanges that clearly increase both sides’ calculators are the most plausible way the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values on red and blue chips, with green chips as a known common-value baseline ($0.50 each). Everyone starts with 10 of each color. From an analyst perspective, tiger-purple-9111 (Tiger) values both red ($0.80) and blue ($1.00) very highly, while duck-purple-4961 (Duck) and lion-yellow-4229 (Lion) value these chips much less and more similarly. This makes Tiger the natural high-value buyer of colored chips and Duck/Lion natural sellers whenever the offered price (in terms of other chips) beats their own marginal value. However, players see only their own values and know only that others’ values lie in the broad public range ($0.10–$1.00), so they cannot perfectly tailor prices to others’ valuations. They instead reason with the surplus calculator (which shows their own gains or losses for each potential trade) and qualitative expectations about others’ likely behavior.

Because each proposal must offer one color and request another, every trade is a bilateral exchange of one chip type for another. The key private tradeoffs are: Duck is nearly indifferent between red and green ($0.50 each) and sees blue as weak ($0.20), so she is willing to part with blue at generous terms but reluctant to give up red or green cheaply. Lion is also relatively close across types (blue $0.30, red $0.40, green $0.50) and values structure and fairness, so he tends to avoid clearly exploitative trades and prefers near-even value exchanges. Tiger, by contrast, gains a lot from acquiring blue and red and is relatively willing to give up green (all green are $0.50, his lowest-value chip). Over the whole game, rational, self-interested behavior would push chips gradually toward Tiger, with Duck and Lion receiving mostly green in return.

Uncertainty about others’ values, combined with simultaneous accept/decline and the random tie-breaking when multiple players accept, creates strategic tension. Proposers cannot control exactly who they trade with and must choose price ratios that are attractive enough to at least one counterpart but not so generous as to sacrifice too much value themselves. Recipients can instantly see whether a proposed trade is profitable in their own dollars, so dominated trades are very unlikely to be accepted. Given that all three participants show relatively high exploitation caution and preference for structure and predictability, we should expect cautious, modestly positive-sum trades that clearly benefit both sides on their own calculators. Early in the game, with symmetric holdings, players will experiment with relatively conservative, approximately fair rates. As the game progresses, observable history (who is accumulating which colors) slightly updates beliefs and may lead to more targeted pricing, but the short 3-round horizon keeps behavior close to static, myopic value maximization. Overall, we expect a handful of accepted trades where lower-value holders sell blue/red to a higher-value buyer (usually Tiger) in exchange for green or a less-valued color, and several declined proposals that are either too one-sided or misaligned with others’ unknown valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has yet observed any trades, so beliefs about others’ valuations are diffuse, guided only by public ranges and vague expectations. Proposers will likely test simple, symmetric-looking offers that feel fair in quantity terms, anchoring on green as a safe common baseline. Because everyone is cautious about being exploited and there is no history yet, trades that visibly sacrifice value for the proposer are unlikely; instead, proposers will aim for slightly favorable yet plausible deals and see whether anyone bites. Recipients will use their calculators and will decline any clearly negative trade. With Tiger likely valuing red/blue more than the others (though they do not know this exactly), early accepted trades are most likely to involve Tiger buying colored chips using green.

By the start of round 1, two trades have executed. Duck has converted some low-value blue into green by trading with Tiger, and Lion has traded some green for blue with Tiger. Public holdings now show Tiger with slightly fewer green and more blue, Duck with fewer blue and more green, and Lion with more blue and fewer green. These patterns suggest Tiger is willing to give up green to acquire colored chips, hinting (from Duck and Lion’s perspective) that Tiger may value red/blue relatively strongly. This encourages Duck and Lion to think of Tiger as a potential buyer and to structure proposals that sell blue or red to Tiger at slightly favorable rates. Tiger, seeing that both Duck and Lion were willing to accept modest gains, will likely continue offering near-even or slightly generous exchanges of green for colored chips, targeting the player whose holdings make the offer most attractive. However, all three remain wary of large imbalances, so most proposals will stay close to value-neutral for the proposer and modestly profitable for at least one recipient.

Going into the final round, the history shows a consistent pattern: Tiger repeatedly buys red and blue using green, and Duck and Lion are generally willing to accept trades that clearly increase their own dollar value. Holdings are now somewhat skewed: Tiger has accumulated extra red and blue at the cost of green, while Duck and Lion have more green than at the start. This public pattern reinforces the belief that Tiger strongly values colored chips, but with only one proposal left per player there is little time for elaborate experimentation. Duck and Lion, both exploitation-cautious and preferring predictable gains, will likely attempt straightforward value-improving trades that sell their remaining low-value blue or red to Tiger at modestly favorable terms. Tiger, aware that this is the last chance to improve his portfolio, will try to acquire additional red/blue without sacrificing much value, again using green as the payment chip. Because all players now have a better qualitative read on each other’s behavior, accepted trades in this round are likely to be clearly positive-sum and moderate in size, with some final declined offers that ask for too much from wary counterparts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips plus common-value green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Red is high for both bear and goose ($1.00 each), medium for ram ($0.80). Blue is very valuable for ram ($1.00), moderately valuable for bear ($0.30), and low for goose ($0.20). Green is identical for all at $0.50 and therefore acts as a natural "currency" benchmark: one green is worth half a dollar to everyone. From a social-surplus standpoint, the most value is created by reallocating blue chips toward ram and reallocating red chips toward bear and goose, while using green chips as a balancing medium when that helps players individually. However, players do not see each other's values; they only know their own and a public range for each chip type. They see an exact surplus calculator for themselves on each proposal and as responders, but must infer others' valuations from behavior. Incentives are purely individual: each player cares only about increasing their own dollar payoff, not group efficiency or fairness. Bear is stable and moderately prosocial but not highly calculating; goose is cooperative, fairly tolerant of unequal outcomes, and less guarded; ram is a strong logical reasoner, patient, inequality-tolerant, and comfortable exploiting arbitrage if he can infer it. The random-recipient rule when multiple players accept introduces competition: proposers must think about setting terms that might attract more than one responder, yet they cannot choose their partner. Across three short rounds, players are unlikely to fully learn each other's precise values, but they may pick up coarse signals: e.g., ram will tend to accept cheap blue-for-red deals that goose or bear might decline, and goose may be lenient on somewhat unfavorable but not terrible trades. The forecasted game below assumes moderately rational but myopic behavior: players use their own calculator, rely on intuitive fairness and rough inferences, and occasionally leave some surplus unrealized rather than perfectly exploiting all cross-player value differences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, holdings are symmetric: each player has 10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN. No one yet has behavioral information about others. From bear's own perspective, red is best ($1.00), green is decent ($0.50), and blue is weak ($0.30). From goose's perspective, red dominates blue even more strongly ($1.00 vs $0.20), while green sits in the middle. From ram's perspective, blue is best ($1.00), then green ($0.50), then red ($0.80). Rationally, big surplus lies in blue flowing toward ram and red flowing toward bear/goose, but with no information, most early proposals will look moderate and symmetric rather than sharply exploitative. Given their personalities, goose and ram are both relatively inequality-tolerant and not strict enforcers, so they may accept mildly disadvantageous trades if they look fair or strategic. Bear is patient and stable but less analytical, likely to make a relatively simple, near-fair first offer using green as a currency.

Entering round 1, holdings have shifted. After round 0 trades: Bear traded away 3 BLUE for 2 GREEN, leaving him a bit more balanced between colors but still heavily red-favoring in value terms. Goose, more inequality-tolerant, accepted that mildly loss-making trade and then offered 5 BLUE for 2 RED, which ram accepted because it was strongly favorable to him given his high BLUE value and only moderate RED value. This reveals to ram that goose is willing to sell BLUE cheaply and values RED a lot; it also slightly signals that BEAR did not compete for that BLUE, hinting he may not value BLUE highly either. For goose, both trades subtly confirm that others find BLUE attractive; but she may not infer exact magnitudes. Ram now sits with a BLUE-heavy portfolio and fewer RED and GREEN; Bear has slightly more GREEN; Goose has significantly fewer BLUE and slightly more RED. In round 1, players start to test more directional, value-exploitative trades, with ram in particular looking for further BLUE purchases or cheap RED-to-BLUE swaps, while bear and goose probe red-for-green or red-for-blue against ram.

By round 2, the pattern is clearer. Goose has repeatedly sold BLUE cheaply and bought RED, revealing she values RED much more than BLUE and is tolerant of giving others good deals. Ram has consistently accepted trades that move him toward more BLUE and fewer RED/GREEN, confirming his high BLUE valuation and comfort with exploiting opportunities. Bear has quietly improved his position in RED and GREEN via small, roughly fair-appearing trades, without extreme moves. Now the game is in its last round, so there is no further benefit to signaling cooperativeness. Proposals will be more opportunistic: each player uses their remaining chips to seek one or two last improvements. Ram, with the most accurate inferred picture of others' values, will especially push for BLUE and try to unload less-valuable RED; goose is still relatively accepting of near-fair trades, while bear avoids large calculated gambles given his low cognitive-reflection pattern and desire for stability.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips, with green chips being common-value at $0.50. Octopus values both red and blue at $1.00 per chip, making them the strongest buyer for both non-green colors. Lion values blue at $0.70 and red at $0.50, so lion is a modest net buyer of blue when red can be given up near cost. Ram values blue at $0.70 but red only at $0.20, so ram is strongly willing to dispose of red in exchange for blue, and moderately willing to give up low-value red to obtain common-value green. Because everyone values green at $0.50, green-for-green trades are irrelevant, and green only matters as a medium of exchange when a player has low-value chips (like ram’s red) that they can upgrade. The main surplus opportunities are: (1) ram selling red to octopus for blue (a large efficiency gain since red is worth $1.00 to octopus but only $0.20 to ram, while blue is $1.00 to octopus and $0.70 to ram), (2) lion selling red to octopus for blue (smaller but still positive gain: red $1.00 vs $0.50, blue $1.00 vs $0.70), and to a lesser degree (3) ram selling red to lion for blue. Octopus, being generous and inequality-tolerant with low exploitation caution, will likely propose trades that share surplus somewhat evenly and may accept mildly disadvantageous trades if they seem cooperative. Lion is cooperative and reflective but somewhat exploitation-cautious, so she will avoid clear losses but may accept modest gains. Ram is resource-protective and less generous, so he will resist trades that don’t clearly improve his own payoff and may decline proposals that offer small or uncertain gains. Strategic uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own private valuations, so they must infer others’ values from offers and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals will be exploratory and relatively conservative, with players testing where others’ willingness to trade lies. Over three rounds, as players observe who accepts which terms, they can adjust toward more efficient trades, particularly around reallocating ram’s low-value red and lion’s medium-value red into blue for themselves, with octopus often acting as the main counterparty. However, simultaneous, private acceptance and random partner selection when multiple players accept means proposers cannot perfectly target specific counterparts, which encourages offers that are at least weakly beneficial for any possible responder.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players have symmetric 10–10–10 holdings and only know their own chip values. They are likely to use round 0 to probe demand for different colors. Lion, being cooperative and reflective, will try a modest, low-risk trade that slightly improves her position in blue without risking a strong loss if accepted. Octopus, who highly values both red and blue at $1.00 and is generous with low exploitation caution, is inclined to experiment with acquiring red from others even at only moderate surplus. Ram, being resource-protective and strongly disliking low-value red, will likely test the market by offering some of his red for blue on terms that are clearly favorable to him, declining offers that do not obviously improve his payoff. Because nobody yet knows others’ valuations, most offers will be simple and small in scale, seeking to discover who is willing to part with which color.

After round 0, octopus has learned that ram is willing to trade away red for green, confirming that ram likely values red relatively low and is using green as a safe upgrade. Lion observed both players decline her even-swap red-for-blue offer, suggesting that at least one other player values either red more or blue less than she does, so perfectly symmetric trades may not clear. Ram observed that his favorable red-for-blue offer was declined, implying that either potential partners value red highly, value blue highly, or both. In round 1, players slightly update their beliefs: octopus will focus on acquiring more red from both partners, using green or blue to pay; lion will experiment with a more generous deal that asks for blue while offering red on somewhat better terms, testing whether someone is a net buyer of red; and ram, still guarding his resources, may attempt a more conservative red-for-blue proposal or pass up marginal deals, erring on the side of no trade rather than risking a loss.

By round 2, trade patterns have clarified: octopus has successfully bought red from both lion and ram on terms that were strictly beneficial to all sides, confirming to everyone that octopus is a strong red-buyer and likely values red highly. Lion has upgraded some red into more valuable blue, and ram has converted some of his particularly low-value red into blue and green. With only one round left, proposers become more focused on extracting any remaining surplus rather than probing. Lion, now quite confident that octopus is eager for red and flexible, will propose a final red-for-blue trade that slightly favors her but is still profitable for octopus. Octopus, having already gained significantly from earlier trades and being generous and inequality-tolerant, may make one last moderate red-acquisition attempt that is still acceptable to a red-holder but not so aggressive that it is rejected. Ram, resource-protective and having seen several of his own offers declined, is unlikely to propose a risky or finely balanced exchange; if he does propose, it will be modest and clearly beneficial to him, but counterparties who now recognize their own advantage in blue may decline, leading to a final round where some potential surplus remains unrealized.

Cat and Duck both value red at $1.00, while Buffalo values red at only $0.60. Blue is especially valuable for Cat ($1.00), moderately valuable for Duck ($0.70), and low-value for Buffalo ($0.10). This creates strong two-sided gains from trade: Buffalo wants to dump blue and, to a lesser extent, red in exchange for green; Cat wants to convert green into blue and red; Duck wants to convert green into red and blue. However, each player only knows their own private values, so they cannot see how good a deal might be for others. They do, however, see their own surplus change for any proposed or received trade via the calculator, which lets them avoid personally negative trades. Because the proposal order is fixed (Buffalo then Cat then Duck in each round) and there are only three rounds, players are under mild time pressure to secure profitable trades but not so desperate as to accept losses. Given the personas, none of them is a strict fairness enforcer; all are inequality-tolerant, with Buffalo and Duck especially adaptive socially. This means a player will accept highly favorable trades without worrying much about whether the proposer also benefits or whether the outcome seems unequal. Buffalo’s stability and uncertainty aversion make her conservative: she will avoid complicated or marginal trades and likely stick to clear, safe improvements. Cat, who values both red and blue at $1.00, has the strongest arbitrage position: she starts off with the most to gain by purchasing low-value colors from others in exchange for green. Duck has good numerical skills and is willing to exploit obvious gains, but his shorter time horizon means he’ll be inclined to take immediate positive trades rather than hold out for a speculative better deal. Overall, we expect early probing offers, some declined because they appear as losses to recipients, and then a few clearly positive, simple trades—particularly involving Cat converting green into red or blue from Duck—being accepted. Buffalo is more likely to offload low-value blue for green or red if she can find a partner whose calculator shows a positive net gain as well.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone is still at their initial endowments (10 of each chip). No one knows others’ exact values, so proposals act as exploratory probes. Buffalo moves first and, given her uncertainty aversion and relatively low value for blue, is likely to test a modest trade that improves her position without being obviously extreme. Cat then follows, with strong incentives to trade away green for red or blue at favorable rates; she’ll likely propose a trade that clearly increases her own surplus, hoping at least one of the others also sees a personal gain. Duck, going last, will react to whatever information the prior offers reveal. He values both red and blue more than green and may try to capture surplus from whoever is most willing to part with them, especially if a previously declined offer hinted at their preferences.

Going into round 1, Duck has already engaged in one very profitable trade with Buffalo and one with Cat, leaving him richer in both blue and green and poorer in neither in value terms. Cat has converted some green into high-value blue, and Buffalo has successfully dumped low-value blue for green. These early successes signal to players that simple, symmetric-looking rates (like 1:1 or 2:2 in different colors) can be mutually profitable. Buffalo now sees that Duck is a willing counterparty for blue-for-green trades, and Cat sees that Duck is receptive to trades that increase his blue or red. Players will adjust their offers slightly, with Buffalo trying again to offload undesired chips, Cat pursuing more green-to-blue conversions, and Duck probing whether anyone is willing to trade away red or blue at rates still favorable to him. Declines will occur when a proposal clearly reduces the recipient’s surplus, especially if it reverses a known profitable direction from earlier trades.

By the final round, a pattern has emerged: Buffalo can reliably trade away blue for green with Duck at attractive terms; Cat can convert green into blue via Duck; and attempts by Duck to buy red with green at modest rates have been rejected. Duck, now holding even more blue and green, is still seeking red but has learned that neither Buffalo nor Cat will give up red cheaply. With only one proposal left per player, they focus on clearly positive, low-risk offers similar to those that have been accepted before. Buffalo will likely repeat her successful strategy, even if marginal gains are smaller now. Cat, seeing repeated acceptance of her green-for-blue deals, will persist with another small, favorable swap. Duck might try once more to buy red using green, but given past refusals and unchanged underlying values, both Buffalo and Cat will probably decline again, ending the game with no last-minute red trades.
