parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so its trades are zero-sum in quantity but neutral in value. The main bargaining action centers on privately valued RED and BLUE chips. Butterfly and Squid both value RED and BLUE highly and fairly similarly (Butterfly: RED $1.00, BLUE $0.90; Squid: RED $0.90, BLUE $0.80). Kangaroo values both low, especially BLUE ($0.10) and RED ($0.40), so she is naturally a supplier of RED/BLUE to the others. Efficient gains come from moving RED and BLUE away from Kangaroo toward Butterfly and Squid, while GREEN can serve as a neutral or slight value sweetener. However, nobody observes others' private values, so early moves are exploratory and driven by simple arbitrage logic: sell low-value chips for high-value ones according to one’s own valuations. Butterfly, as a stable, logical, fairly prosocial type, is likely to propose mutually profitable but slightly self-favoring trades and to avoid obviously losing deals. Kangaroo is cautious and protective, so she will avoid trades that visibly reduce her own payoff and may only accept clear, simple gains. Squid is analytically strong and socially adaptive, willing to coordinate if it is profitable and not obviously exploitative. A large strategic uncertainty is that players cannot choose which of multiple acceptors they trade with; if both non-senders accept, the partner is chosen at random, so proposers must design offers that they are happy to execute with either party. Over three short rounds, most bargaining will focus on straightforward two-chip trades (RED↔BLUE or RED/BLUE↔GREEN) that show positive surplus for both sides, with some early hesitation as each player feels out whether the others also regard RED and BLUE as high-value. The likely path is a modest number of accepted trades that steadily move RED and BLUE away from Kangaroo toward Butterfly and Squid, with GREEN occasionally used as payment by Butterfly and Squid to avoid giving up their own high-value RED/BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any trading behavior yet, so proposals are conservative and diagnostic. Each player knows their own valuation gaps but not others'. Butterfly and Squid both suspect that at least one other player may value RED and BLUE differently, and Kangaroo knows she values BLUE especially low. Early offers are likely to be simple, small quantities that are clearly profitable to the proposer and look at least plausibly acceptable to unknown counterparts. Risk-averse, structure-seeking personalities will shy away from big, complex gambles; instead, they test market appetite with modest, nearly fair or slightly self-favoring offers.

After round 0, some information has leaked via observed trades. From Butterfly’s first successful offer, others can infer she values BLUE more than GREEN. Kangaroo’s acceptance of BLUE-for-GREEN and her own BLUE-for-GREEN offer to Squid reveals that she is willing to shed BLUE cheaply, while Squid’s acceptance indicates BLUE is also valuable to her. Squid’s declined attempt to buy RED for GREEN, however, suggested that neither Butterfly nor Kangaroo is willing to give up RED at close to 1:1 with GREEN, hinting that RED may be valuable to both. In this second round, proposals adjust: Butterfly and Squid are more confident that BLUE is cheap for Kangaroo and may seek larger BLUE purchases, and Kangaroo knows she can reliably move BLUE for GREEN at a gain. RED becomes the contested, high-value chip everyone is reluctant to sell without strong compensation.

By the final round, a strong pattern is established: Kangaroo is the primary supplier of BLUE and sometimes RED; Butterfly and Squid are primary demanders of BLUE and RED and pay mostly with GREEN. All three have seen consistent acceptance of trades where Kangaroo gives up BLUE for GREEN and, more recently, of a trade where Kangaroo gives up RED for BLUE when the BLUE return is sufficiently high. This gives everyone a clearer sense that Kangaroo’s valuations for both colored chips are lower than theirs. With only three proposals left, Butterfly and Squid will try to squeeze in last profitable exchanges, especially for RED, but remain unwilling to sell much of their own RED or BLUE. Kangaroo, recognizing her pattern, continues to monetize excess BLUE and some remaining RED for GREEN or BLUE from others whenever that yields her a clear positive calculator surplus. The uncertainty now is minimal; most remaining disagreement is on how steeply RED should be priced relative to BLUE and GREEN in these last opportunities.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip-bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so green chips are a transparent, neutral "money-like" asset. Red and blue chips are private-value: bat values both red and blue at $0.80, fox values blue highly ($0.90) and red low ($0.40), and frog values blue highest ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.50). All players know the public value range for red and blue but not the exact values for the others. This creates strategic uncertainty: everyone can see that blue is probably more valuable than red on average, but no one is sure who values which color most. Given the personas, all three are cooperative, inequality-tolerant, and not strongly punitive, so they are unlikely to sacrifice money to enforce fairness norms. Instead they lean toward mutually beneficial trades that are clearly positive in their on-screen surplus calculators. Green, being common-value, will likely function as a safe medium for experimenting with trades: proposing swaps of green for red/blue where the proposer is clearly gaining and suspects the counterparty might also see a gain given typical value ranges. Blue is likely to be the most contested color because all three players personally value it at least as much as red; frog in particular has a very strong intrinsic incentive to accumulate blue. Bat, with strong numeracy and high cooperation, is likely to propose clear win–win trades that slightly favor her but remain attractive to at least one partner. Fox is also cooperative and logical, inclined to make and accept modestly advantageous trades without over-optimizing. Frog is structure-seeking and moderately cautious; they will avoid negative-surplus deals but accept any obvious positive swap that helps concentrate value in high-value chips (especially blue) and move out of lower-value chips. Because acceptance decisions are private and multiple acceptances go to a random recipient, there is some residual risk when a sender designs a deal that would be highly beneficial with one partner but not the other: the wrong partner may accept. However, many natural trades here are positive-sum for both possible recipients given the spread in valuations. Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, we are likely to see an initial probing phase with small green–red or green–blue trades, followed by larger, more targeted proposals as they infer patterns from who accepts what. Given shared cooperativeness, the game is likely to realize several accepted trades that move red chips toward bat (who values red strongly) and blue chips toward fox and especially frog, with green flowing in the opposite direction as a balancing asset.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one knows others’ exact red/blue values, but everyone knows green is uniformly worth $0.50. The first round is a natural exploration phase: players can test the market with small, obviously profitable offers using green as a safe reference. Bat, as an early proposer and numerate cooperator, is likely to propose mild, clearly positive deals that she expects at least one counterpart to accept. Fox will then see one piece of information—the fate of bat’s proposal—and may try a slightly bolder but still fair-looking trade. Frog, proposing last, can best observe both previous acceptance patterns and use his structure-seeking tendencies to craft a targeted trade that increases his blue or sheds low-value red. Because everyone is tolerant of inequality and not strongly punitive, the main constraint is strictly negative-surplus deals shown in red on the calculator, which they will avoid.

By the second round, the group has already revealed some information through which trades went through. Frog accepted giving up green for red with bat, signaling he does not view green as especially superior to red at the margin. Bat accepted turning green into blue with fox, suggesting she values blue strongly, perhaps comparable to red. Fox accepted giving blue for red with frog, revealing that she is not desperate to hoard red but is comfortable trading blue away for a modest gain. These patterns hint that everyone likes blue and that red–green–blue tradeoffs can be mutually profitable. In round 1, players can now pursue somewhat larger or more targeted trades: bat may try to continue upgrading into her favored colors, fox may trade green for blue or swap lower-valued red into assets others value more, and frog will seek more blue while offloading relatively less-valued red. Still, given their cooperative but inequality-tolerant dispositions, they focus on clear individual gains rather than squeezing each other.

By round 2, the players have observed multiple accepted trades: green-for-red, green-for-blue, and red-for-blue exchanges. Each player has a clearer view of which colors others are willing to give up, though they still lack exact numeric values. Bat and fox both appear comfortable swapping green and red for blue when the terms show them a personal surplus. Frog has shown high demand for blue and willingness to part with green and red when the deal looks positive. Given their stable, cooperative tendencies and the finite horizon of this third and final round, they now focus on last opportunities to convert low-value holdings into high-value ones. Purely exploitative offers that impose losses on counterparts will be automatically screened out by the surplus calculators, so the likely realized trades remain modestly sized, individually beneficial swaps that continue moving blue toward frog and fox and red/green toward bat and the others as appropriate.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 chips of each color (RED, BLUE, GREEN). GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so there is no gain from trading GREEN for GREEN; it only functions as a medium that can be used to infer others’ valuations or as a concession to make a mixed bundle seem more acceptable. The private values for RED and BLUE differ: dog-green-8079 values RED=$0.50 and BLUE=$0.30; parrot-pink-5400 values RED=$0.80 and BLUE=$1.00; turtle-red-9966 values RED=$0.60 and BLUE=$1.00. This means RED and BLUE are both more valuable for parrot and turtle than for dog, and BLUE is especially valuable for parrot and turtle but relatively weak for dog. Bilaterally, dog can profit by selling RED or BLUE to parrot or turtle in exchange for GREEN (or, in principle, by getting a large number of BLUE from them if they misprice it, but that is unlikely once they see their calculator). Parrot and turtle, in contrast, have little incentive to give up their high-value BLUE unless they get even more value in RED or GREEN. The main surplus opportunities are: (1) dog trading away BLUE (low value for dog, high for others) for GREEN, and (2) dog trading away some RED for GREEN when partners are willing to overpay in GREEN relative to dog’s valuation. However, each player only sees their own values, so ex ante they are uncertain about the others’ valuations and must infer them from which trades are proposed and accepted. persona-wise, dog and parrot are empathic, inequality-tolerant, and not strict norm enforcers, so they may be flexible about unequal gains and try exploratory offers. Turtle is more guarded against exploitation and has higher minimum fairness thresholds, so she is likely to reject any trade that is even slightly negative or looks too unbalanced. Because acceptance decisions are made with full knowledge of one’s own surplus and because responders cannot condition on who else might accept (they move simultaneously), they will generally accept any strictly profitable trade and reject any loss-making one. The random tie-breaking when multiple players accept makes it risky for proposers to construct offers that are attractive to both partners equally; if dog wants to target one specific partner, he must skew the offer so that the intended recipient benefits more clearly than the other, otherwise he risks trading at a less-than-ideal price with a random counterpart. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), we should see early exploratory, relatively small trades that test demand for RED vs BLUE, then some adjustment as players infer that BLUE is widely valuable and that dog is the relatively weak holder of BLUE. Given dog’s lower exploitation caution and higher social adaptiveness, he is the most likely to propose value-creating trades that give large gains to parrot or turtle in exchange for modest but positive gains for himself. Parrot, being generous but also valuing both RED and BLUE highly, may propose trades that are roughly break-even for himself, hoping to create goodwill or small improvements. Turtle will likely only accept very clear positive deals and may not propose many risky or generous offers, favoring predictable, obviously good trades over speculative ones. Overall, the game is likely to produce a few successful trades centered on dog exporting low-value BLUE (and perhaps some RED) to the higher-valuing buyers, with some tentative GREEN-for-RED or GREEN-for-BLUE offers that are declined when they do not clearly help the responder.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color. No one has any information about others’ valuations beyond the common range hints, so this round is mainly exploratory. Dog, as the first proposer, is the low-value holder of BLUE and may test the market by selling BLUE or RED for GREEN at modest margins, hoping at least one partner will find the deal obviously profitable. Parrot, generous and inequality-tolerant, might make an early offer that gives noticeable gains to a potential partner to establish a cooperative pattern, but he still will not accept losing trades due to the surplus calculator. Turtle, more guarded and fairness-sensitive, is unlikely to accept anything that is not clearly positive and may propose safer, symmetric or near-symmetric trades when it is her turn. Because responders move simultaneously and only one accepter is chosen at random, the proposers must anticipate that overly generous deals could be snapped up by either partner, so they may try to create offers that slightly favor one specific recipient (e.g., by using colors that seem more likely to be valuable to that player) while still being acceptable to themselves.

After round 0, the only executed trade is dog selling 3 BLUE to parrot for 2 GREEN. This reveals several things: parrot is willing to pay GREEN for BLUE, and dog is willing to give up BLUE cheaply, suggesting BLUE might be less valuable to dog and more valuable to parrot. Turtle, observing but having declined all trades, remains a bit on the sidelines and more guarded. Dog now has slightly fewer BLUE and more GREEN; parrot has more BLUE and less GREEN; turtle’s holdings are unchanged. In round 1, dog is likely to build on this by offering more BLUE to either partner for GREEN, possibly improving terms slightly now that he sees clear demand. Parrot, now with a strong BLUE position, may try to rebalance by trading some BLUE for RED if he suspects others may value RED more than he does, or he might seek more RED from dog to exploit a higher private value for RED. Turtle, having remained cautious, now has one data point suggesting BLUE is in demand, and she might either try to buy BLUE cheaply from dog or sell a bit of RED or BLUE for GREEN if she thinks others overvalue GREEN. However, her exploitation caution makes her continue to avoid trades with ambiguous or tiny gains.

By the start of round 2, dog has traded away several BLUE chips and gained GREEN and a couple of RED from parrot; parrot has accumulated BLUE and some extra RED at the expense of GREEN; turtle has just once bought BLUE from dog and otherwise remained conservative. The observed pattern suggests to all that BLUE is generally desirable and that dog is comparatively willing to sell it, reinforcing the inference that dog’s BLUE valuation is relatively low. Parrot has revealed a willingness to exchange GREEN for both BLUE and RED, hinting that his valuations for those colored chips might exceed $0.50. Turtle, after finally accepting a clearly beneficial BLUE-for-GREEN trade, has shown that she will participate if the advantage is obvious. In this final round, dog still has an incentive to unload remaining BLUE at favorable terms, perhaps targeting the partner who seems more responsive or who has more GREEN left. Parrot may try one more rebalancing move, seeking RED or perhaps offering a small amount of BLUE if he believes others overvalue it, though the calculator will keep him from giving up clear surplus. Turtle, with high exploitation caution and a near-final horizon, will likely only accept trades that visibly add net value and may propose at most one more simple, near-fair trade, if any. Because this is the last round, goodwill-building is less relevant, and all players focus on strictly profitable, immediately beneficial exchanges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green-for-green trades never occur and green acts like a neutral currency benchmark. Private values differ on RED and BLUE: Badger (B) values BLUE=$0.90, RED=$0.60; Peacock (P) values BLUE=$0.10, RED=$1.00; Unicorn (U) values BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.40. Thus B and U are high-blue types and low-red relative to P, who is high-red and low-blue. This creates natural gains from trade: P wants to acquire RED and unload BLUE; B and U want to acquire BLUE and are more willing to give up RED. Because all see the same public ranges ($0.10–$1.00) and know valuations may differ, they must infer others’ types from offers and acceptances over a short 3-round horizon. Multi-player acceptance and random recipient selection introduce strategic uncertainty: if both potential partners accept, the proposer cannot target a specific counterpart, so they design proposals that are acceptable to at least one high-surplus partner while still leaving them ahead regardless of which partner is chosen. Personas suggest P is structured, highly analytical, and cooperative, likely to craft value-creating but not self-sacrificing trades. B is somewhat cooperative but uncertainty-averse and intuitive, likely to propose simple, safe trades that visibly benefit her. U is cooperative, inequality-tolerant, and somewhat impulsive, more open to modest gains and less inclined to punish slightly unequal deals. Over three rounds, players will first probe with information-revealing but modest trades, then move toward clearer exploitation of the revealed high-blue/low-blue and high-red/low-red structure, with most surplus generated from blue-for-red exchanges between P and the two high-blue players, while green remains largely untouched except as a safe, visible anchor of value.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip color and knows only their own private values. Strategic focus is on testing counterparts’ likely valuations for RED vs BLUE. Badger and Unicorn privately know they like BLUE more than RED, while Peacock privately likes RED more than BLUE. Each player, however, only has beliefs about the others. Round 0 is therefore exploratory: modest, low-risk trades that signal preferences without giving away too much surplus. Because trades can be accepted by either non-sender, proposals will tend to be designed so that at least one counterpart likely sees a non-negative gain, even if the other might not.

By the start of Round 1, some structure has emerged. From Badger’s first trade, others can infer she is at least moderately willing to swap RED for BLUE. Peacock’s acceptance of that trade reveals that he finds that exchange at worst mildly unfavorable but acceptable, hinting that his RED value is relatively high versus his BLUE value. His own proposal, offering 2 BLUE for 1 RED and being accepted by Unicorn, then strongly suggests to everyone that Unicorn is also a high-blue/low-red type. So by Round 1, the latent structure—Peacock is the high-red/low-blue counterparty to two high-blue/low-red players—is partially revealed to everyone. Now, proposers can aim more confidently at efficient red-for-blue trades while protecting their own surplus. Minor misalignments and cautious behavior still mean some beneficial trades are passed up if they seem too one-sided or if uncertainty about exact valuations remains.

By Round 2, value patterns are fairly clear: Badger and Unicorn are both high-blue/low-red, Peacock is high-red/low-blue, and earlier offers/acceptances support this. Time is short, so players look for last opportunities to convert less-valued chips into more-valued ones, but also avoid proposals that would clearly give away too much surplus or likely be declined. Badger, somewhat uncertainty-averse, will favor simple, low-risk exchanges. Peacock, as an analytical cooperator, will try a reasonable surplus-creating move if available but not at a substantial personal cost. Unicorn, inequality-tolerant and cooperative, may accept modestly favorable deals that lock in small but certain gains. Mis-coordination and slightly misaligned price ratios still leave some potential surplus unrealized, but a few final trades consolidate the main efficiency gains of the high-blue agents trading with the high-red agent.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known red/blue values and common-value green chips. All start with 10 of each chip. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so green-for-green trades never happen, but green often acts as a neutral or safe store of value.

Key asymmetries: owl-purple-3001 values BLUE highly ($0.70) versus RED ($0.50); mouse-green-4201 values BLUE slightly more than RED ($0.40 vs $0.40, actually equal); dog-orange-6603 values RED and GREEN equally at $0.50 and BLUE lower at $0.30. Efficiently, dog should sell BLUE to owl and mouse in exchange for RED or GREEN, and owl should offload some RED for BLUE. Mouse has relatively balanced values and may trade opportunistically but has weak incentives to move chips aggressively.

However, each player only knows their own payoffs. They see that green publicly lies in $0.50–$0.50, but only know that red/blue lie in $0.10–$1.00 for others. They can infer rough preferences from who is eager to buy or sell which color, but the game has only three rounds, limiting learning.

Behaviorally, all three profiles are fairly cooperative and tolerant of mild inequality. Dog is generosity-forward and intuitive, with low exploitation caution and high uncertainty aversion; she may offer clearly win-win trades and avoid complex or risky asks, sometimes giving away too much surplus. Mouse is prosocial but more deliberative and stable; he will seek small but clear gains and avoid strongly exploitative moves. Owl is analytically strong, socially adaptive, and moderately cautious; he likely makes more value-extracting but still acceptable proposals, trying to arbitrage BLUE where he has a strong advantage, while avoiding offers that would predictably be rejected.

Strategically, each proposer wants to craft trades that are clearly positive for at least one counterpart under most plausible valuations. With two potential recipients, proposals that are favorable to any single counterpart risk multi-acceptance; then a random recipient is chosen. Players therefore avoid offering deals that are strongly attractive to both non-senders unless they are roughly indifferent to which recipient they get. Most trades will be simple one-color-for-one-color or small integer ratio swaps that visibly increase the proposer’s value without obviously harming the recipient.

Given only three rounds and three turns per round, dynamics are short-horizon: players cannot rely on long-run reputation. They will typically start modestly in Round 0—testing others’ willingness to buy/sell specific colors—then refine offers in Rounds 1–2 using observed acceptances/declines to infer who likes which colors. Because all three are prosocial and not strict norm enforcers, we expect a few mutually beneficial trades to execute, focused around reallocating BLUE from dog to owl and some RED/BLUE between mouse and owl. Some apparently favorable proposals might still be declined if they look too skewed or confusing given limited information about others’ valuations.

Overall, the likely trajectory: early tentative trade attempts (some declined), then a couple of clearly positive-sum swaps as preferences become clearer, with owl gradually increasing BLUE holdings, dog offloading some BLUE for RED/GREEN, and mouse making at least one mild surplus-improving trade while avoiding big downside risk.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is exploratory. No one yet knows others’ red/blue values, so proposals are small and relatively safe. Dog, as a generous but uncertainty-averse first mover, will likely offer a mildly favorable deal for at least one counterpart, testing demand for BLUE or RED. Mouse will respond by proposing a straightforward value-increasing swap once he sees how others react, and owl will use both prior reasoning and the just-seen trades to start nudging the allocation toward more BLUE for himself without appearing exploitative.

By Round 1, players have some signals: mouse showed willingness to trade RED for BLUE with owl, and dog signaled she is happy to give up BLUE for GREEN. Owl’s attempted GREEN-for-BLUE trade was declined, hinting that others may not see GREEN as strictly inferior. Now, proposals become slightly more targeted. Dog will again try to move low-value BLUE for higher-value RED or GREEN, mouse may exploit his central position between dog and owl to pick up incremental surplus, and owl adjusts away from GREEN-for-BLUE deals that others rejected, instead offering RED for BLUE where he has strong comparative advantage.

Round 2 is the final opportunity to adjust holdings. Players now have a clearer picture: dog repeatedly sells BLUE, owl repeatedly buys BLUE and sells RED, and mouse is willing to trade in both directions when gains are modest but clear. With limited time remaining, they focus on simple, clearly positive trades. Dog may attempt one last BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN move, mouse decides selectively whether further tweaks are worth it, and owl will try a final arbitrage of his remaining RED into more BLUE if a counterpart still sees value in RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED more than BLUE, and GREEN is a constant $0.50 for everyone. Alligator and Buffalo have identical valuations: RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.70, GREEN=$0.50. Rabbit is slightly weaker on both colored chips: RED=$0.90, BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50. This creates relatively modest but clear gains from trade: any deal that moves chips from Rabbit to Alligator/Buffalo in exchange for GREEN can generate surplus, because Rabbit is effectively the "low-valuation" type. However, because players do not know each others' precise values, they must infer who is likely to be high or low value through proposals and accept/reject behavior. The main tension is between exploiting small arbitrage gains vs. keeping the interaction simple in only three rounds. Green is common-value and known to be stable at $0.50, so it serves as a natural medium of exchange and anchor for proposals. The surplus calculator makes it easy for each player to see whether any posted deal is personally profitable, so irrational acceptance is unlikely; the uncertainty is mostly about whom a given proposal will appeal to and whether multiple acceptances will cause random partner selection. Personality-wise, Alligator is analytical, patient, and relatively inequality-tolerant, so he will focus on clearly positive-expectation trades without worrying much about fairness optics. Buffalo is prosocial and generous but also dislikes uncertainty; she is likely to propose simple, clearly profitable terms and readily accept modest gains rather than hold out for perfect deals. Rabbit is also prosocial and reflective, with low norm-enforcement and low exploitation caution, so she is willing to take small positive deals even if they are a bit skewed against her, and may occasionally post offers that are only marginally beneficial to herself in the hope of facilitating exchange. Over three rounds, we expect a few small GREEN-for-RED/BLUE swaps, with Rabbit often on the less advantageous side but still improving versus no trade, and limited multi-player competition for any one proposal because valuations are fairly close.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, everyone begins with symmetric holdings (10 of each chip) but only knows their own payoffs. They share a common prior that RED and BLUE are likely between $0.10 and $1.00 and GREEN is reliably $0.50. Reasonable first-round strategies are exploratory yet conservative: test simple one-for-one or small-ratio trades to reveal interest without risking big losses. Alligator is likely to start by trying to acquire more RED using GREEN or BLUE, since his RED is the most valuable chip. Buffalo, being cooperative, will likely propose a trade that is clearly beneficial for others as well as herself, such as offering GREEN for a mix of colored chips. Rabbit may be a bit more willing to post a deal that is very slightly negative for her expected value if she misjudges, but with the calculator showing her net change she will still avoid outright losses. The biggest uncertainty in this first round is who seems eager to accumulate which color, which will inform later proposals.

By the start of round 1, Rabbit has already sold several RED to Alligator and Buffalo for GREEN, revealing that at least she is willing to part with RED at something like 3 GREEN for 2 RED. Alligator and Buffalo infer that Rabbit’s private value of RED is probably not extremely high. Rabbit in turn infers that both others see RED as attractive, since they bought from her on identical terms. This pattern encourages Alligator and Buffalo to continue targeting Rabbit as a likely counterparty for future RED trades, and may also nudge Rabbit to experiment with BLUE-for-GREEN offers if she wants to rebalance away from an excess of GREEN. All three now have more information, so proposals are more targeted: Alligator and Buffalo try to get slightly more favorable terms on RED and possibly BLUE, while Rabbit looks for ways to trade away surplus GREEN or to acquire some higher-value chips without giving up too much.

Going into the final round, Rabbit has offloaded several RED and some BLUE in exchange for GREEN and sees that both Alligator and Buffalo consistently accept trades where they receive RED or BLUE for GREEN at moderate ratios. Alligator and Buffalo are ending up with more of the higher-valued colored chips, while Rabbit is accumulating GREEN and may feel somewhat behind but still better off than with no trade at all. With only three proposal turns left, players become more opportunistic: they focus on any last positive-margin trade rather than experimenting. Alligator is content with his portfolio and proposes a slightly more favorable deal to test if Rabbit will still sell RED cheaply; Buffalo looks for one more BLUE acquisition; Rabbit, recognizing she may be short on colored chips, tries once more to get RED from the others but faces tighter acceptance because Alligator and Buffalo now value their remaining RED and BLUE more given the impending end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 to everyone) and private-value red and blue chips. Goose values red at $0.90, blue at $0.50; Bear values both red and blue at $0.50; Buffalo values red at $0.80 and blue at $0.20. All start with 10 of each color. Green is a neutral currency; all private gains come from exploiting differences in red/blue values. Goose and Buffalo both value red more than blue, while Bear is indifferent between red and blue. That creates strong incentives for Goose and Buffalo to accumulate red and unload blue; Bear can intermediate trades across players, being roughly neutral about which color they hold. However, players only know their own valuations, not others’, so they must infer relative values from which colors others try to buy or sell. The UI shows each player their own net gain/loss for any posted trade, so no one will knowingly accept a negative-surplus trade; proposals must leave at least one counterparty non-negative in their own terms. Because all three are somewhat inequality-tolerant and not harsh norm enforcers, they are unlikely to sacrifice certain gains just to punish others, but their medium exploitation caution means they reject trades that look like clear losses. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn others’ values, so many offers will be conservative, trying to ensure that at least one responder sees a small positive surplus. Goose, being a strong reasoner and patient, is likely to probe with structured offers that trade away some blue or green for red, testing demand. Bear, empathic and somewhat intuitive, may aim for “fair-looking” 1:1 or 2:1 swaps that yield small, safe gains and are easy for others to accept. Buffalo, reflective and numerate, is likely to spot the arbitrage opportunities between their low blue value and higher red value, and may also respond flexibly to serve as a trading partner when offers are favorable. Uncertainty remains over who values which color, whether two players will simultaneously accept and create random partner selection risk, and how quickly players realize that blue is systematically undervalued by Buffalo but not by Goose or Bear. The most likely pattern is that modest, roughly balanced trades occur early, with slightly more targeted and mutually beneficial exchanges emerging as players observe each other’s revealed preferences across turns.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still hold their initial endowments and have no direct evidence about others’ values. Proposals will likely be simple and conservative, with 1:1 or 2:1 ratios that are easy to evaluate visually and unlikely to impose big losses. Goose is apt to start by trying to acquire red, since red is clearly their highest value chip, perhaps offering green or blue because those are relatively less special to them. Bear, who values red and blue equally, might attempt a modest trade to test if others favor one color over the other, aiming for very small but safe gains. Buffalo is likely to experiment with selling blue (low personal value) for red or green, but will also be attentive to any pattern in others’ acceptance behavior that hints at their private values. Because everyone is cautious, it is plausible that the first proposal or two are declined if they look even slightly unfavorable in the responders’ own calculators, with at most one trade actually executing this round.

By the start of round 1, two trades have already occurred, revealing partial information. Goose traded away three blue for two red to Bear, signaling that Goose values red more than blue and is willing to pay a premium in blue to get red. Buffalo traded four blue for two red with Bear, also indicating a strong preference for red over blue. Bear, by accepting both trades, has shown indifference or mild tolerance to exchanging between red and blue at roughly 1:1.5 or 1:2 ratios, suggesting Bear’s private values for red and blue are quite similar. After these moves, Bear holds more blue and less red than initially, while Goose and Buffalo both hold more red and fewer blue. Players can reasonably infer that red is broadly coveted and that Bear is a flexible counterparty, especially for blue-for-red or blue-for-green exchanges. In this round, Goose may try to fine-tune their portfolio further by offering blue or green to obtain even more red from Bear, though Bear’s red stock is more limited now. Bear might seek to rebalance back toward red if they infer that others value red more highly, potentially charging a mild premium in blue for red. Buffalo, now more confident in the pattern, may look for another opportunity to offload remaining low-value blue for red or green, but will avoid trades that overly deplete their blue if they anticipate later leverage. Small, low-risk trades are still favored, but with more targeted offers that exploit the now-clear pattern that both Goose and Buffalo value red strongly.

Entering round 2, the final round, players have a clearer picture of relative preferences. Goose has repeatedly tried to acquire red, and Bear has often been the counterparty, signaling that Bear is willing to move red for blue or green when the rate is not too unfavorable. Buffalo’s early trade, exchanging blue for red, plus their acceptance of a blue-for-red offer from Bear, shows a consistent pattern: Buffalo is very willing to give up blue for red and only cautious when the rate looks too steep. Bear, after several exchanges, likely holds a relatively blue-heavy mix and fewer red chips than initially, while Goose and Buffalo have accumulated more red and shed blue. Because this is the final set of proposals, there is no future signaling value; players focus purely on immediate payoff. Goose may attempt one more small-value trade that marginally increases red holdings at acceptable cost in blue or green, but must respect that others will reject any net-loss trade. Bear may try a last-minute arbitrage trade where they see a clear positive spread in their own calculator, especially swapping surplus blue for green or red from the others. Buffalo is likely to look for one final favorable blue-for-red exchange but may also decline offers that barely break even, given their strong numeracy and exploitation caution. With only three turns left, some offers that are slightly unbalanced may still be posted in hopes that at least one counterpart sees a modest gain, but overall, trades will remain conservative and only execute when both sides clearly benefit given their private values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN), but with different private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is a fixed common-value $0.50 chip for everyone. Privately, Peacock values RED highest ($0.80) and BLUE at $0.50; Cat values RED and GREEN equally ($0.50) but BLUE lower ($0.30); Otter values RED at $0.70, GREEN at $0.50, and BLUE lowest at $0.20. So for all players, GREEN sits at or near the middle, BLUE is weakest on average (especially for Otter and Cat), and RED is strongest overall (especially for Peacock and Otter). This creates clear comparative-advantage patterns: Otter has strong incentive to dump BLUE for RED or GREEN, Peacock wants to turn BLUE into RED or GREEN but is happy holding RED, and Cat is most indifferent between RED and GREEN but wants to avoid accumulating too much BLUE. Incentives differ across potential trade pairs: gains are largest when Otter moves BLUE to others in exchange for RED, or trades BLUE away for GREEN; Peacock similarly benefits from trading BLUE for RED or GREEN but has smaller gains when swapping RED away (since RED is their best chip). Cat has moderate incentives to sell BLUE to either partner for RED or GREEN, and weaker incentives to move RED because they value it the same as GREEN. Uncertainty comes from incomplete information: no one knows others’ exact valuations, so a player proposing a trade must guess which colors are more or less valuable to others. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be conservative, exploiting obvious safe improvements (e.g., swapping some BLUE for GREEN at a rate that’s personally profitable but not outrageously one-sided). As histories accumulate, everyone can infer broad patterns: if someone readily parts with BLUE but fights to keep RED, then RED is probably high-value for them. Persona cues suggest all three are cooperation-oriented, patient, and prefer structured, predictable arrangements; none is a strong punitive enforcer. That makes it more likely that they look for win–win patterns rather than extreme exploitation, especially where simple mutually beneficial trades are easy to identify with the surplus calculator. However, the random-recipient rule when both non-senders accept means proposers cannot target a specific partner: any over-generous deal might accidentally give surplus to the partner they infer values that chip less. This reduces the willingness to post overly generous proposals that could be snapped up by the “wrong” counterpart. Over three rounds, with each player proposing three times, the most likely arc is: (1) Round 0: cautious tests, especially simple BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED swaps that clearly help the proposer; some proposals get declined if they obviously hurt the responder given their calculator. (2) Round 1: more directed proposals exploiting revealed willingness to move certain colors, with at least one substantial accepted trade reallocating BLUE away from Otter and Cat in favor of more RED/GREEN. (3) Round 2: final optimization, with smaller or more balanced trades as marginal gains shrink and holdings are already somewhat specialized. Across the game, accepted deals will typically be modest, positive-surplus-for-both trades that align with the broad value structure: BLUE flows out of Otter and Cat and into Peacock (who values it relatively more than they do) or is swapped into RED/GREEN, while RED tends to accumulate with Peacock and Otter, and GREEN floats as the neutral medium allowing incremental improvements.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip, has seen no behavior yet, and knows only their own values. With high uncertainty and a shared preference for structured, low-risk action, proposals are likely to be simple and conservative. Each proposer will probably treat GREEN as a safe intermediate chip (known mid-value to them) and BLUE as relatively weak, especially for Cat and Otter. Because no one has yet observed others’ trades, they cannot target proposals tightly; instead they trial low-stakes exchanges that visibly give themselves positive surplus without being blatantly exploitative. Given simultaneous private responses, non-senders will typically accept any proposal that the calculator shows as a non-trivial gain and reject clear losses. In this first round, we expect at least one modest but clearly mutually beneficial trade to be accepted as players probe the market for exchanging BLUE into better chips.

By Round 1, Otter has shed some BLUE in exchange for GREEN in Round 0, and both Otter and Peacock have revealed a willingness to trade BLUE for GREEN at roughly a 3-for-2 rate. Cat has not yet made a proposal that reveals strong preferences, but has seen that Otter eagerly accepts chances to dump BLUE. Players infer: (1) BLUE is likely low-value to Otter; (2) GREEN is acceptable to everyone; (3) Peacock is comfortable paying with BLUE to gain GREEN. As private calculators guide decisions, proposers now experiment with slightly richer trades that try to convert their least-valued chips into RED or GREEN, anticipating that Otter in particular will keep offloading BLUE. However, the random-recipient risk if both non-senders accept still encourages moderated terms. This round is likely to feature at least one successful trade giving Otter more RED for BLUE, and one cautious but accepted trade between Cat and Peacock that nudges both toward portfolios better aligned with their valuations.

Entering Round 2, BLUE has been steadily moved away from Otter and, to a lesser extent, from Cat, in exchange for GREEN and RED. Otter now holds a portfolio tilted toward RED and GREEN, consistent with her incentives; Peacock has increased GREEN and possibly some RED via earlier trades; Cat has picked up extra RED and parted with some BLUE and GREEN. With only three turns left, marginal gains from further rebalancing shrink. Players now have better qualitative beliefs: everyone sees that Otter is quick to trade away BLUE and eager for RED, that Cat is fine trading BLUE for RED or GREEN at moderate rates, and that Peacock is selective about RED but willing to swap BLUE for GREEN. Final proposals are likely to be smaller and more balanced, aiming for low-risk incremental improvements rather than big swings. Some deals that would benefit only one side are quickly rejected thanks to the calculators; the accepted trades, if any, tend to be mild Pareto improvements, often involving BLUE being exchanged for GREEN where both sides see slight surplus. At least one last accepted trade rebalances remaining BLUE into more preferred chips, while other ambitious attempts to grab RED at cheap rates are declined.

Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so trades involving green mainly reallocate common surplus or are used as a simple, transparent currency. Red and blue create the strategic tension: badger values both red and blue at $1.00; squid values blue at $1.00 but red only at $0.10; camel values blue at $0.80 and red at $0.40. This structure creates large gains from trade when chips move from low-value to high-value holders, especially red moving away from squid to badger, and some potential gains moving blue from camel to squid or badger. Because recipients respond privately and multiple accepts lead to a random partner, proposers cannot perfectly target the highest-value counterparty and must design offers that are attractive enough for at least one other player while not being so generous that they forgo surplus. Personas suggest all three are relatively cooperative, logical, and stable. Squid is highly analytic and empathic, likely to search for Pareto-improving trades and be willing to concede small surplus to ensure agreement. Badger has stronger norm-enforcement tendencies and may resist trades that feel exploitative or overly one-sided, but also values efficiency when offers are clearly beneficial. Camel is cooperative, trusting, and somewhat less guarded, willing to accept modest but positive gains. Across three short rounds, players face uncertainty about each other's private values and about who will accept any given proposal, but they see a surplus calculator from their own perspective. This encourages them to accept any trade with positive personal gain and reject negative ones. Proposers will tend to converge on simple, obviously beneficial patterns: squid selling red for blue or green, badger buying red with blue or occasionally green, and camel making or accepting moderate positive-surplus trades without over-optimizing. With only three rounds, there's limited scope for elaborate signaling; instead we expect a small number of executed trades that move red into badger's hands and blue into squid's or badger's, with at least one plausible but slightly misaligned offer being declined when another player perceives insufficient surplus or feels wary of overtrading.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each color and has no information about others' private red/blue values beyond the common ranges. Proposers will likely begin with cautious, simple trades involving small quantities, testing how willing others are to trade and which colors draw acceptance. Squid will want to unload low-value red for higher-value blue or at least neutral green. Badger, valuing both red and blue highly, may initially try to buy red cheaply, perhaps using green or blue. Camel, knowing their moderate valuations and cooperative bent, is likely to make or accept trades that yield clear but not extreme gains. No one yet knows who's the red lover (badger) or the red hater (squid), so early offers are exploratory and somewhat conservative.

By round 1, observed behavior has revealed partial information. Squid's initial sale of five red for two blue signaled that red is relatively low value to him and that blue is valuable. Badger's quick acceptance at a clear personal gain indicates he values red strongly and is willing to exchange blue for it. That trade also revealed to camel that red might be more valuable to at least one other player than to squid. Badger's failed attempt to buy red with green showed that neither squid nor camel wants to give up red just for common-value green at a one-for-one rate, hinting that their red valuations are not extremely low. Camel's declined attempt to swap green for blue suggests the others are not desperate to shed blue for green. With this limited history, squid will likely continue to trade away red, perhaps for green now that they have fewer blue chips. Badger may refine his offers, possibly using blue again to buy red but in smaller or safer quantities. Camel, now more informed, may focus on modest positive-sum trades, possibly involving red and blue, where he can capture small gains without sacrificing too much optionality.

Entering the final round, substantial information has accumulated. Squid has repeatedly offered to sell red for blue or green, signaling a strong preference against red and in favor of blue and at least neutrality toward green. Badger has aggressively accumulated red, paying with both blue and green, confirming his high private value for red and tolerance for parting with some blue and green to get it. Camel accepted a trade where he gave up red for blue at a favorable rate, showing that he values blue noticeably more than red but still treats red as somewhat useful. By now, everyone can infer directional preferences: squid dislikes red, badger likes red a lot, and camel likes blue more than red. With just three turns left, proposers will aim for clearly positive-sum, simple trades that lock in remaining gains from trade without worrying much about future signaling. Squid may try one last small unload of red to either other player. Badger, now rich in red and somewhat poorer in blue and green, might make a more balanced or even conservative final offer. Camel may either try a small trade to marginally increase blue or green, or decline to propose overly complex or risky swaps this late in the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading privately valued red and blue chips against a common-value green chip in just three short rounds. Everyone starts with 10 of each color, and each player only knows their own chip values. Green is worth $0.50 to all, but red and blue values differ: lion values both red and blue at $0.20, iguana values red at $0.50 and blue at $0.70, and alligator values red at $0.30 and blue at $0.70. From an analyst’s perspective, efficient surplus creation comes primarily from moving blue chips away from lion (who values them least) toward iguana and alligator (who value them more), and secondarily from moving red chips from lion to iguana, with smaller gains from moving red from lion to alligator. Green has no private variation, so it mostly serves as a neutral medium for trades or as an anchor when players are unsure about relative values. Because players do not see others’ private valuations, they must infer who likes what through proposals and acceptances, all while knowing they cannot choose the specific recipient when multiple players accept. The proposer schedule is fixed: lion then iguana then alligator in each round. Lion is patient, numerate, and tolerant of inequality, which makes her likely to initiate seemingly generous but self-beneficial offers, especially early, and to accept modest positive trades without worrying about strict fairness. Iguana is reflective and inequality-tolerant with medium exploitation caution, so she is open to profitable deals and may strategically anchor on preserving green unless the gain in red/blue is clear. Alligator is reflective, empathic, more cautious about exploitation, and dislikes uncertainty, so she is less likely to accept ambiguous or thin-margin trades, especially ones that might signal she is being used as a dumping ground for low-value chips. Throughout the game, the surplus calculator gives each player clear, immediate feedback about their own gain or loss from every possible deal, so all accept/decline decisions are individually rational in terms of their own payoffs. The main uncertainties are about others’ valuations and future proposal opportunities: players know they only have three rounds of three proposals, and they must balance waiting for better deals against the risk that no one will offer them a clearly beneficial trade later. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be relatively simple, exploratory exchanges using green as a safe counterpart; as history unfolds, players can infer that lion is likely offloading red/blue, while iguana and alligator are willing buyers for blue and, to a lesser extent, red. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, proposers must craft deals that are attractive to at least one target without being too generous to the other possible respondent. That randomness encourages symmetric-looking offers that can plausibly be accepted by either high-blue player. Over three rounds, we expect a few accepted trades that transfer blue (and some red) away from lion toward iguana and alligator, with iguana slightly more willing than alligator to take on riskier or thinner-margin deals, while alligator’s higher exploitation caution makes her selective and sometimes leaves mutually beneficial but uncertain trades on the table.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still have the symmetric starting endowments, and no one has yet learned anything about others’ private values. Proposals are likely to be exploratory and relatively conservative, often using green as the payment side because it has a known common value. Lion moves first and, as a numerate, inequality-tolerant player with low exploitation caution, is willing to propose a trade that gives the responder a visible surplus while still improving her own payoff, expecting this will both be accepted and provide information about others’ preferences over colors. Iguana and alligator, not yet knowing that lion values red and blue relatively low, will judge offers primarily via the surplus calculator. Iguana, reflective and medium-cautious, will accept trades that are clearly profitable even if they look slightly lopsided. Alligator, being more cautious about exploitation and preferring predictability, will decline deals that either involve large swings for unclear gain or that feel like she is being used as a dumping ground for low-value chips, especially if an offer seems asymmetrical in favor of the proposer. Because there is no trade history yet, proposals in this round may be somewhat symmetric in chip counts, and responders may err on the side of declining very thin-margin or ambiguous-looking trades.

By round 1, the accepted trade from lion to iguana has revealed that lion was willing to give up multiple blue chips in exchange for green, and that iguana saw buying blue with green as worthwhile. This suggests, from an analyst’s view, that lion likely values blue relatively less than green, while iguana values blue at least as much as, and probably more than, green. Alligator, having declined both her own and others’ thin-margin or ambiguous offers in round 0, appears cautious. The updated holdings give iguana more blue and fewer green, lion fewer blue and more green, and alligator remains at the initial endowment. Lion, recognizing that blue is easy to sell to iguana, is now more confident in designing surplus-generating trades that offload remaining relatively low-value colored chips, potentially experimenting with red as well. Iguana now has a better sense that green is in demand from lion and that blue is something she can profitably accumulate; she also realizes alligator is selective and may not easily accept medium-margin deals. Alligator, still cautious, may look for deals that let her upgrade her red holdings (which she values somewhat) or acquire blue if she can pay with green without feeling exploited. Overall, round 1 is characterized by more targeted offers, mainly aimed at iguana as a willing buyer, with alligator continuing to filter out offers that do not clearly beat her current bundle.

In round 2, the final round, lion has successfully converted several relatively low-value colored chips into green via trades with iguana, and iguana has accumulated a larger portfolio of red and blue at the cost of giving up some green. Alligator has not yet engaged in any accepted trade, reflecting her higher exploitation caution. The short horizon (this is the last set of proposals) makes players more willing to entertain slightly thinner margins as there is no benefit to waiting for future, potentially better offers. Lion, still with favorable opportunities to offload remaining low-value red or blue, may attempt one more targeted offer aimed at iguana, likely using a structure similar to earlier trades that have been accepted. Iguana, knowing she has benefited from earlier trades, will look for one last profitable exchange but may also be more selective if it overly depletes her remaining green. Alligator, recognizing it is the final chance to improve her position, may be somewhat more open to modest positive trades than before, though still avoiding anything that yields only negligible or ambiguous surplus. Random selection among multiple acceptances, if it occurs, could slightly distort final allocations, but given alligator’s history of declining medium-margin offers, many final proposals are effectively bilateral between lion and iguana, with alligator either declining or facing deals that are not clearly favorable on her surplus calculator. Overall, round 2 consolidates the pattern: lion continues to trade away lower-valued colored chips primarily to iguana in return for green, while alligator exits with holdings close to her starting bundle.
