RED and BLUE are private-value: zebra values RED highly ($0.80) and BLUE moderately ($0.30); gorilla values BLUE moderately ($0.40) and RED low ($0.20); turtle values BLUE very highly ($0.70) and RED very low ($0.10). Thus, efficient trades move RED from gorilla/turtle to zebra and BLUE from zebra/gorilla to turtle, often using GREEN as a neutral or mildly costly medium of exchange. Because every player sees only their own values, they know they like or dislike each color but must infer others’ preferences from observed behavior. The UI surplus calculator means no one will knowingly accept a trade that reduces their own payoff, but they can and likely will accept small positive gains even if the other side benefits more. Random recipient selection when multiple players accept means senders are incentivized to design offers that could be attractive to more than one counterpart without being too generous; however, all three personas are relatively cooperative and tolerant of unequal outcomes, so they are willing to leave significant surplus with partners as long as they personally gain. Zebra is generous and structure-seeking, likely to initiate clearly beneficial but somewhat partner-favorable trades early to establish predictability. Gorilla is stable, cooperative, and patient, likely to search for reasonable, low-friction surplus trades without pushing hard on bargaining power. Turtle is logically strong and somewhat guarded, inclined to accept any strictly positive, especially BLUE-acquiring, trades and to design proposals that extract good value once she infers others’ patterns. Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, they have limited time to converge on rich multi-step exchanges, so we expect a few simple, bilateral swaps: early exploratory offers in Round 0, then more targeted, value-aligned trades in Rounds 1–2 as they infer that zebra values RED, turtle values BLUE, and gorilla sits somewhat in the middle using GREEN/BLUE/RED more flexibly. Some profitable trades will fail because the sender mis-pitches the price or addresses the wrong partner, but with these prosocial profiles, several surplus-enhancing trades should still go through.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has 10 of each chip type. No one yet knows how others value RED and BLUE, but they know GREEN is common-value and that RED/BLUE lie in [0.10, 1.00]. Early proposals are likely to be simple and somewhat conservative, serving as both value-extraction attempts and information probes. Zebra, as first proposer and structure-seeking, is inclined to make a modest, clearly positive-sum offer that she suspects at least one player might accept, probably giving up some BLUE (which she values least) or asking for RED (which she values most). Gorilla, second, will respond after seeing zebra’s initial behavior and may tentatively exploit any perceived price slack, but given her cooperative nature, she is more likely to propose a moderate RED-for-BLUE or RED-for-GREEN trade. Turtle, going last, observes both previous offers and responses, and—being a strong reasoner—starts inferring who likes which color. She highly values BLUE, so she will likely hunt specifically for opportunities to acquire BLUE, either by proposing to buy BLUE with her low-value RED or by accepting any reasonable BLUE-gaining proposals.

By Round 1, several signals have emerged. Zebra’s first trade revealed her willingness to part with BLUE for RED at a fairly generous rate, hinting she values RED more and BLUE less. Gorilla’s acceptance and subsequent RED-for-GREEN trade suggests she does not value RED highly and is comfortable giving it up for GREEN, possibly to balance her portfolio or in anticipation of later trades. Turtle’s BLUE-acquiring move, paying generously with GREEN, strongly signals that BLUE is especially valuable to her. Everyone now has rough beliefs: zebra likes RED, turtle likes BLUE, gorilla is flexible and somewhat price-sensitive but willing to trade when she gains. With this shared but still noisy understanding, Round 1 proposals should be more targeted: zebra will try to buy RED cheaply from gorilla or turtle (likely from gorilla, who has shown low attachment to RED); gorilla may attempt to arbitrage between zebra and turtle, using her BLUE and GREEN; turtle will try either to buy more BLUE or to unload some of her excess low-value RED, now that her strong preference for BLUE is on the table.

Entering the final round, patterns are fairly clear: zebra has repeatedly bought RED with BLUE, gorilla has consistently sold RED and occasionally GREEN for modest gains, and turtle has eagerly bought BLUE even at sizeable cost in GREEN and RED. Each player now faces a last opportunity to fine-tune their portfolios. Zebra, with strong structure-seeking and generosity, is inclined to propose a final, moderately generous RED-acquisition offer, especially toward gorilla, who has shown willingness to part with RED. Gorilla, behaviorally stable and cooperative, is likely to look for one more safe, low-risk surplus-increasing trade, possibly unloading remaining low-value RED or trading BLUE if the price is right. Turtle, confident that BLUE is her high-value asset and RED her low-value one, will either attempt one more BLUE purchase or accept any final proposal that offers her extra BLUE or at least a small net gain in dollars. Because this is the last round, there is no reputational or future strategic concern beyond immediate payoffs, but their cooperative leanings mean they still avoid exploitative or highly lopsided offers that might be perceived as unfair or confusing, focusing instead on clear, individually beneficial swaps.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values to each player, while green chips are common-value and thus strategically neutral aside from inventory. Each player starts symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color), but asymmetric in how much value they attach to red vs. blue: cat-green-4185 values blue highly relative to red (0.70 vs. 0.50), iguana-yellow-3262 values red highly relative to blue (0.80 vs. 0.30), and zebra-pink-2457 values blue highest but also likes green and is indifferent to slightly weaker red (0.80 blue vs. 0.40 red). Efficient trades exploit these differences: iguana wants to accumulate red and dump blue, while cat and zebra want to accumulate blue and may be more willing to give up red. Since green has the same value for everyone (0.50) and is publicly known to be common-value, its main role is as a neutral medium in early feeler trades or as a way to offer something that is visibly fair, though it doesn’t create pure surplus in the same way as mismatched red/blue values. Uncertainty comes from the fact that each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others’ preferences from what they propose and accept. Because responders see exact dollar gains/losses for themselves when evaluating a proposal, they will reject any strictly negative offer and are likely to accept modest positive gains even if it looks slightly unbalanced, especially given that all three have relatively low norm-enforcement tendencies and moderate-to-high cooperation orientation. Behavioral stability and cognitive ability are highest for cat and iguana; zebra is cooperative but somewhat less analytical. The likely overall dynamic is that cat, as a logical reasoner and first mover each round, tests the waters with modest, nearly value-neutral trades that feel fair and minimize rejection risk. Iguana, highly exploitation-cautious and structure-seeking, will tend to propose trades that slightly but clearly improve her own payoff while still leaving a small carrot for others, especially trades exchanging her low-value blue for red. Zebra, patient and cooperation-oriented but less calculating, is likely to respond generously to clear personal gains and propose simpler, intuitively fair trades, occasionally giving up some surplus if the structure seems mutually beneficial. Across the three rounds (nine total proposal opportunities), we should expect a few accepted trades that gradually move red chips toward iguana and blue chips toward cat and zebra, while most green-for-color feelers either get taken only when they offer a small visible gain or get declined once players infer better directional preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and have no information about others’ private values. Players will likely use early proposals as probes. Cat, a strong logical reasoner with moderate generosity and low norm enforcement, is likely to start with a cautious, nearly value-neutral offer that looks fair in chip counts and uses green as a safe medium. Iguana, reflective and exploitation-cautious, will look for trades that move her toward more red and away from blue without risking obvious exploitation. Zebra, more cooperative but less numerate, will be responsive to clear personal gains and may try intuitive red-for-blue swaps that look mutually helpful. Since no one yet observes revealed preferences, early proposals may be declined if they give responders little or no visible surplus.

By the start of round 1, iguana and zebra have executed one trade: iguana gained 2 red and gave up 3 blue, while zebra lost 2 red and gained 3 blue. This reveals that iguana is willing to pay blue for red and that zebra is at least somewhat happy to accept blue for red at that rate, signaling that zebra probably values blue more than red. Cat has seen this but has not traded yet, and may infer that both others see some advantage in moving red toward iguana and blue toward zebra. With these signals, proposals now become more targeted: cat may try to move red to iguana or blue to zebra; iguana will continue to push for red while dumping her undervalued blue; zebra, newly flush with blue and short on red, may either replenish red in cheap ways or further exploit blue’s high personal value.

Going into round 2, a series of accepted trades have already pushed the system in the efficient direction: red has moved steadily toward iguana, blue toward zebra (and somewhat to cat), and green has been shuffled between iguana and zebra in roughly value-neutral ways. Everyone now has clear evidence that iguana likes red and discards blue, zebra likes blue and is willing to part with red, and cat is prepared to trade red for blue when it is at least slightly beneficial. With only three proposal turns left, players become somewhat more aggressive in seeking final marginal gains. Cat, still holding some red, may try one last red-for-blue swap with iguana at a slightly improved personal rate. Iguana, facing reduced blue inventory, may use green as payment for any remaining red she can obtain at positive surplus. Zebra, seeing strong revealed preferences, may attempt a small blue-for-red trade that remains personally profitable while offering iguana a modest gain. Reply decisions now are almost purely calculator-driven, with each player accepting only those offers that show a clear positive dollar change, given the limited remaining time.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Private values: Bear values BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.70; Peacock values BLUE=$0.40, RED=$0.60; Swan values BLUE=$0.90, RED=$0.50. BLUE is relatively high for Bear and Swan, relatively low for Peacock. RED is moderately valuable for Bear and Peacock, lower for Swan. Given they only see their own values, each player knows which colors are personally attractive but does not know who else also likes them.

Key incentives:
- Bear: BLUE is best, then RED, then GREEN. Efficient trades for Bear involve giving up GREEN (and sometimes RED) to gain BLUE. She will rarely give up BLUE and will be cautious about dumping RED unless she gets a lot of BLUE or at least high-value color in return. She is structured and stable, so she is likely to search for clearly positive deals and avoid confusing, risky proposals.
- Peacock: RED is more valuable than BLUE and GREEN, but his BLUE is especially weak. Efficient trades for him involve offloading BLUE or possibly GREEN to gain RED. He is exploitation-cautious and not very generous, so he will avoid taking losses and prefer clearly self-beneficial moves, but may be open to modest win–wins.
- Swan: BLUE is most valuable, RED is least. Efficient trades involve trading away RED or possibly GREEN to gain BLUE. She is numerate and reflective, so she will read the surplus calculator carefully and avoid negative EV offers.

Because partner selection is random whenever multiple players accept, proposers cannot precisely target a specific partner. That pushes them toward deals that would plausibly be acceptable to more than one player, or at least to one likely counterparty they can infer from behavior. Early in the game, information is thin: each player only knows their own values and sees anonymous accept/decline patterns from others. Over turns, acceptances and rejections provide weak signals about others’ preferences (e.g., if someone consistently accepts BLUE-for-RED, they likely value BLUE more than RED).

Green has the same value to everyone and sits in the middle of the various personal valuations, so GREEN often acts as a safe, neutral currency to bridge value gaps. However, time is limited to three rounds (nine total turns), so there is incentive to take immediately positive trades rather than wait for perfectly tailored partners. Given all three have modest fairness enforcement and medium exploitation caution, they will mostly accept any trade with nontrivial positive surplus and reject negative ones, with minimal concern for group efficiency or equality. The likely outcome is a modest reallocation of chips where Bear and Swan gradually accumulate more BLUE and slightly fewer RED/GREEN, while Peacock shifts marginally toward more RED (if opportunities appear), but overall changes are incremental due to the short horizon and random matching risk.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone is at the symmetric endowment (10 of each color) and has no information about others’ valuations. Proposers will likely try simple, obviously self-profitable trades involving GREEN as a neutral currency or swapping BLUE for RED based on their own ranking, without yet knowing who values what. Accept/reject patterns this round mainly serve as weak signals of others’ preferences. Risk of random recipient selection, if both non-senders accept, will discourage extremely asymmetric deals that could benefit only one specific partner.

By round 1, some information has emerged. Swan has just accepted a trade giving up RED for BLUE, revealing a clear personal preference for BLUE over RED. Bear accepted a GREEN-for-BLUE trade, signaling she also likes BLUE more than GREEN. Peacock observed that his BLUE-for-RED offer appealed to Swan but not Bear, suggesting Bear already values BLUE highly and is reluctant to give it up, whereas Swan is an eager BLUE buyer. Proposers can now more credibly target Swan for BLUE-demanding trades and Bear for GREEN-for-BLUE opportunities, while avoiding trying to buy BLUE from Bear. Still, each proposer must contend with random partner selection risk if both other players like the deal.

Entering the final round, everyone has clearer signals: Bear has repeatedly tried to acquire BLUE using GREEN, Swan has consistently accepted trades that increase her BLUE holdings at the cost of RED or GREEN, and Peacock has sought more RED but found limited uptake when the terms slightly disadvantage others. Chip distributions are now somewhat skewed: Bear and Swan have accumulated extra BLUE, while Peacock has relatively more RED and fewer BLUE. With only three turns left, players focus on any remaining, obviously positive marginal trades, especially those that use GREEN or low-valued colors (from their own perspective) to gain higher-valued ones. They are less likely to risk complex or thin-margin offers that might be rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN, with GREEN worth $0.50 to everyone. The strategic tension comes from private values for RED and BLUE: gorilla and monkey both value RED at $1.00, but BLUE is cheap for gorilla ($0.10) and very valuable for monkey ($0.90). Camel values BLUE moderately ($0.60) and RED poorly ($0.30). This creates natural surplus trades where gorilla and camel can cheaply supply BLUE to monkey in exchange for RED, and where camel wants to turn low-value RED into higher-value BLUE. However, players only know their own valuations, so they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. Gorilla is patient, numerate enough, and dislikes uncertainty; she is likely to propose conservative, clearly-profitable trades and to avoid large speculative moves until she sees patterns in responses. Camel is very analytical and inequality-tolerant, with low generosity: he will focus on extracting surplus for himself and is comfortable proposing trades that are only weakly beneficial (or even slightly costly) to others if he thinks at least one partner is sufficiently motivated by their own gains. Monkey is reflective and prosocial, more willing to accept mutually beneficial trades and to occasionally leave some surplus to others if it helps coordination. The surplus calculator makes each accept/decline decision myopic and precise: no one need guess whether a given offer is personally profitable, only how it affects future bargaining power. Early in the game, proposals double as learning signals about relative preferences (e.g., whether someone readily trades away RED or BLUE). Later rounds see more targeted attempts to exploit known comparative advantages. Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner assignment, proposers must balance making trades attractive enough to get at least one acceptance while avoiding making them so attractive that both partners jump in and introduce lottery risk over which counterpart they get. With only three rounds, the game is short: players are unlikely to fully reveal valuations, and each proposal is primarily optimized for immediate surplus with moderate attention to subsequent bargaining positions.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone still has symmetric endowments and only their own valuations. Gorilla, being structure-seeking and cautious about uncertainty, is likely to open with a small, clearly-profitable trade that tests demand for BLUE versus RED or GREEN. Camel, as a strong logical reasoner who guards his own resources, will quickly target trades that improve his RED-to-BLUE mix, trying to turn low-value RED into higher-value BLUE even if he has to spend some GREEN. Monkey will respond in a more prosocial and exploratory way, willing to accept moderate personal gains even if they seem generous to the proposer, but still guided by his calculator. Their early moves mainly serve as cheap experiments to infer others’ relative valuations, while preserving flexibility for later rounds.

By the start of round 1, the only executed trade has been between camel and monkey, where monkey gave up some BLUE for camel’s low-value RED. That strongly suggests to both gorilla and camel that monkey values BLUE highly relative to RED. Camel infers that monkey is an especially good partner for RED-for-BLUE swaps, while gorilla learns that someone in the group (likely monkey) will pay generously for BLUE. Gorilla now wants to unload more of her low-value BLUE in exchange for RED, but will frame proposals cautiously after her initial failure. Camel, having just improved his position by turning weak RED into better BLUE, will look for a follow-up trade that pushes further in the same direction, perhaps involving GREEN if necessary. Monkey, now holding extra RED and slightly fewer BLUE, wants to rebuild his BLUE stock but will avoid overpaying, especially after realizing he made camel a very good deal; his reflective nature makes him update acceptance thresholds upward.

By the final round, the history makes the pattern of comparative advantage fairly clear. Gorilla has repeatedly tried to trade between RED and BLUE and saw that monkey, not camel, is most eager to acquire BLUE, while camel has aggressively transformed RED into BLUE whenever possible. Gorilla, still preferring secure gains and closure, will use her last proposal to capture a modest improvement without risking large inventories of low-value BLUE. Camel, recognizing this is the last chance, will push for a strong personal gain, even if it offers only thin margins to others, but may find partners resistant after earlier generous deals tilted in his favor. Monkey, aware that he has already done well in value terms but is short on BLUE relative to his ideal mix, will look for one last efficient swap that increases his BLUE holdings at reasonable cost. Because there is no continuation value beyond this round, all three focus purely on the immediate surplus from their final proposals, and unattractive trades are simply declined rather than strategically accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-value green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and all can trade only by offering one color to buy another, with trades executing if at least one counterparty accepts. Because each player sees only their own chip values, they must infer others’ valuations from observed proposals and acceptances. The strategic core is exploiting value differences: alligator values blue highly and red very low; otter values blue highest and red moderately; tiger values both colors positively but especially red. Green is mid-value and common-value, so it serves as a neutral or safe medium of exchange. Incentives push players to turn low-value chips into high-value ones while avoiding trades that reduce their own total value. Uncertainty comes from not knowing whether others value red or blue more, which affects whether a superficially attractive trade will be accepted or declined. Empathic, reasonably cooperative tendencies mean players often avoid blatantly exploitative offers, but they still prioritize personal gain. Across three rounds, early turns are exploratory—testing whether others will move red for blue or vice versa—while later turns adjust based on observed refusals and accepted trades. The random assignment of a trading partner when multiple players accept also matters: players may slightly shade offers toward being acceptable to both counterparts, knowing they can’t target a specific partner. Over the full game, we expect modest gains from trade, a few declined attempts where potential counterparts’ valuations are misjudged, and convergence toward clearer red-for-blue price patterns once someone reveals willingness to move one color for another.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip, with no direct information about others’ private red/blue valuations. Given their personas and access to the surplus calculator, early offers are likely to be cautious and mildly favorable to the proposer, probing whether others are willing to trade away red or blue. Green, known to be worth $0.50 to everyone, is a natural reference point but not ideal to give up for much more valuable chips unless the proposer misreads others’ values. Alligator, as a patient, logical reasoner, will likely start with a small, positive-value probe rather than an aggressive exploitative offer. Otter, reflective and structure-seeking, will try to secure blue or red at a cost that still yields a clear gain for herself. Tiger, who is short-horizon but prosocial and logically capable, will respond strongly to visible gains, accepting offers that clearly improve his value even if they somewhat favor the proposer.

By round 1, players have seen some willingness to move red and blue, giving partial information about others’ likely priorities. Alligator has signaled he is happy to trade away red for blue, which hints that red is relatively low-value to him; otter has traded away some blue for red, indicating she values red non-trivially; tiger has shown eager acceptance of blue-for-red trades but declined selling red for green, signaling that red might be particularly precious relative to green. These signals reshape expectations: offers that ask others to give up their most-valued color (blue from otter, red from tiger) must be generous to be accepted. Players now try to refine their portfolios: alligator still wants more blue and fewer reds; otter wants to improve her blue stock but at good terms; tiger wants red but would also accept good blue-for-red or blue-for-green opportunities if profitable. Personality-wise, prosocial tendencies and low norm-enforcement levels mean they avoid obviously one-sided trades but will still walk away from small or negative gains.

Entering the final round, each player has a clearer but still imperfect picture of others’ values. Alligator has repeatedly acted as if blue is far more valuable than red or green, and he has accepted trades that reduce red for modest gains in blue or even small improvements in red. Otter has shown that she is willing to trade red and green but guards blue more carefully, consistent with blue being particularly valuable to her. Tiger has accepted blue-for-red trades and avoided giving up red cheaply, reinforcing his strong valuation of red. With only three turns left and no future bargaining leverage to preserve, each proposer now focuses on clearly positive, relatively simple offers that lock in incremental gains. Because they’ve learned rough price-like ratios for red, blue, and green through the earlier acceptances and rejections, they are less likely to propose blatantly unacceptable offers and more likely to hit mutually beneficial trades when possible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-chip-type bargaining game with private values for red and blue and common value for green. Everyone starts with 10 of each chip. Green is known to be worth $0.50 to all players, so it functions as a stable numéraire and safe store of value. Red and blue are privately valued: dolphin values both cheaply ($0.10 each), frog values both highly but especially red (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.50), and pig is intermediate (RED $0.40, BLUE $0.30). The main potential gains from trade come from reallocating red and blue chips away from dolphin toward frog and pig, while compensating dolphin with green. Frog, as the player who values red the most, has strong incentive to acquire red from dolphin, ideally paying with blue (which dolphin undervalues) or some green. Pig has medium valuations and can both buy from dolphin and sell to frog, possibly acting as an intermediary if proposals and randomization allow. However, players only see their own values, plus a surplus calculator for any proposed trade from their own perspective, so they reason under uncertainty about others' valuations. Dolphin, being structure-seeking and cooperative, is likely to make clear, modestly generous offers early that visibly give each potential partner a small surplus, rather than pushing very hard for maximum extraction. Frog is prosocial and socially adaptive but relatively tolerant of inequality, so she is likely to accept any strictly beneficial trade and propose mutually beneficial ones, especially to obtain red. Pig is somewhat impatient and intuitive, willing to take uneven but profitable deals and less focused on fine-tuned optimization. Because at most one responder is randomly selected when multiple accept, senders cannot target a specific partner; this discourages extremely tailored proposals and encourages offers that are attractive to at least one other player but not so generous that both are always eager to accept. Across three short rounds, most surplus gains will likely be realized in the first two rounds, as opportunities are clear (dolphin’s low-value red and blue) and the players are cooperation-oriented. By the final round, remaining opportunities are smaller, and risk of overpaying with green or high-valued red/blue grows, so we expect more cautious or declined proposals at the margin.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each chip. The key early uncertainty is who values which colored chips. Dolphin, with low red and blue values and strong structure-seeking preferences, is likely to test the waters with a simple, clearly beneficial offer that gives away some red or blue in exchange for green. Frog and pig will use these early proposals to infer that dolphin likely values the traded chip less. They will accept any offer that clearly increases their own surplus and reject those that require giving up too much green relative to perceived benefit. Because everyone still has symmetric endowments and little history, early-round trades are most about discovering where the gains from trade lie and building confidence that accepting profitable but slightly unequal deals is safe.

After round 0, frog has already revealed a strong willingness to buy red and to pay with blue, while pig has shown readiness to trade when the calculator shows clear profit. Dolphin’s first trade signaled that she is comfortable exchanging red for green at a low implicit red value. The updated holdings now have dolphin with more green and fewer red, frog with more red and fewer blue plus slightly fewer green, and pig with more blue and fewer red. In this middle round, players can refine their strategies: frog will try to consolidate more red from both dolphin and pig without giving away too much green; pig may opportunistically arbitrage between what he can obtain cheaply from dolphin and what frog is willing to pay; dolphin will continue to offload red or blue if she can gain green, but may become slightly more selective as her red pile shrinks. Because value gaps for red are tighter between frog and pig than between dolphin and the others, trades of red from dolphin remain the highest-margin opportunities.

Going into the final round, most of the large, obvious gains from trade have been realized. Dolphin has shifted substantial red and blue into green, indicating strong preference for the common-value chip; frog has accumulated a larger red pile at the cost of some blue and green; pig has taken advantage of some spreads but has also parted with red to frog. At this stage, each player’s remaining potential gains are smaller and more marginal. Dolphin is unlikely to give up additional green without clear compensation, frog must be careful not to sacrifice too much green or red chasing a few extra low-valued chips, and pig may prefer to hold rather than risk ending worse off. With only one proposal per player left and no future rounds, the shadow of the future is very short: players will accept only clearly positive surplus trades, and proposers may test slightly ambitious offers that are more likely to be declined. As a result, we expect at most one more mutually attractive trade, and some proposals that look profitable for senders but unappealing for at least one responder will now be rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 to everyone) and privately valued red and blue chips. Owl strongly prefers red ($1.00) and likes blue ($0.60); Squid values red and blue somewhat less ($0.90 and $0.50); Iguana values both red and blue relatively low ($0.30 and $0.20). All three value green identically at $0.50. This structure makes Owl the most eager buyer of red, and a relatively strong buyer of blue; Iguana is a natural seller of both red and blue; Squid sits in the middle but has somewhat higher values than Iguana, especially for blue. Because players observe only their own values, they don’t know exactly how strong others’ preferences are, only that greens are safe anchors. They can, however, see all past trades and holdings. Incentives: (1) trade low-value chips for high-value chips for oneself, (2) avoid trades that visibly create large gains for others when offers could be improved, and (3) act quickly when an obviously favorable trade appears, because the other non-sender can also accept and might be randomly chosen. Given the personas, Iguana is a cautious, numerate optimizer who avoids giving away too much surplus; Owl is inequality-tolerant and intuitive, willing to cut simple, decent deals without insisting on precision; Squid is empathic and socially adaptive, likely to accept clearly positive deals and to propose balanced trades that feel fair. Across three rounds, everyone has three proposal turns, so they can experiment early and refine later. A likely pattern is: early probing offers close to value-neutral for the proposer (testing what others will accept), then more targeted offers where Iguana sells blue to the higher-value buyers, Owl buys red even at a premium, and Squid both arbitrages moderate gains and sometimes accepts slightly inferior-to-ideal trades for the sake of closing a deal before the game ends. Uncertainty remains about how aggressively to price offers given random recipient selection when both others accept, and about how strong others’ preferences are over red versus blue.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has seen others’ behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ values are diffuse, except for knowing green is common-value. Round 0 is mostly about information-gathering and establishing baselines. A logical Iguana is likely to make an early, safe test offer that is slightly favorable but not extreme. Owl and Squid, both relatively inequality-tolerant, will accept modest gains but may decline anything that looks too lopsided or hard to interpret. Because future rounds exist, players need not grab every small surplus; they can wait for better options once they infer who really wants red and blue.

By the start of round 1, players have seen that Owl is willing to pay with blue to acquire red (Owl’s 2 RED-for-3 BLUE deal with Squid), revealing that Owl values red strongly but is at least somewhat cost-sensitive on blue. Squid has shown willingness to accept a modest but clear gain. Iguana’s first declined probe suggests others won’t trade green for blue at par when they can likely get better deals. With these signals, Iguana can infer that red is in demand and that green is a neutral yardstick. Now, proposals are more targeted: Iguana seeks to offload low-value red to the players who’ve indicated demand; Owl continues to try to accumulate red and may use green or blue depending on who they target; Squid tries to capture surplus without risking being stuck with unwanted colors.

Entering the final round, everyone has some evidence: Owl has consistently bought red using both blue and green; Squid has shown readiness to accept trades with clear positive gains, often involving blue and green; Iguana has mostly tried to move red and green to reposition into colors that might be underpriced from their perspective. With only three turns left, the shadow of the endgame makes players more willing to lock in any remaining gains, even if not perfectly priced. Iguana, patient and calculative, now makes one last attempt to convert disliked red to better-valued chips. Owl, focused on red and tolerant of inequality, makes an aggressive final grab for red even at a premium, trusting their high valuation. Squid, socially adaptive and somewhat more risk-tolerant in gambles, balances between accepting good last-minute offers and avoiding trades that obviously overpay for already-costly red.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This is a three-player bargaining game with three chip colors whose dollar values differ by player for red and blue but are common and known for green. Every player starts with 10 of each color, and green chips are always worth $0.50 to everyone. Red and blue chips are within a publicly known range ($0.10–$1.00), but each player only knows their own exact values: Gorilla values red highest (0.80) and blue medium (0.50), Llama values blue highest (0.80) and red medium (0.60), Whale values both red and blue relatively low (0.60 and 0.30, respectively). All see past trades and holdings, but not others’ private values.

Incentives: All three benefit from trading away low-value chips for them in exchange for higher-value chips. Gorilla has a strong motive to accumulate red and is willing to give up blue or green at a discount; Llama wants blue and is more willing to give up red or green; Whale has the lowest blue valuation and moderate red valuation but is the most cooperative, so Whale is willing to accept modest gains that also help others. Green is neutral/common-value at $0.50 each, so it often plays the role of a safe currency or balancing chip; trades involving green are less informative about private values than red-for-blue or similar.

Dynamics and uncertainty: None of the players can directly choose partners—if multiple others accept, a recipient is randomly chosen—so proposals must be attractive to at least one counterpart, without being so generous that both rivals eagerly accept unless the proposer is comfortable with randomized partners. Because each player sees only their own valuation schedule, they must infer others’ preferences from which trades are accepted or declined. Gorilla is structure-seeking and somewhat risk-averse, so expects to make relatively cautious, clearly self-profitable proposals emphasizing red as the desired chip. Llama is behaviorally stable and somewhat self-protective, likely to avoid any negative or very small-gain trades but willing to quickly grab good deals on blue. Whale is prosocial and less guarded, ready to accept low but positive surplus trades and to extend slightly generous offers, even when not maximizing surplus.

Over three rounds (nine proposal turns total), bargaining is likely to converge quickly toward a red–blue pattern: Gorilla trying to buy red (sell blue/green), Llama trying to buy blue (sell red/green), and Whale flexibly bridging gaps with green and low-valued blue. Early in the game, accepted trades will gradually reveal that Gorilla loves red, Llama loves blue, and Whale is easiest to deal with. That learning feeds back into later rounds, where proposers may actively target Whale with modest but safe gains and use slightly more aggressive surplus-seeking offers when proposing to the more guarded Gorilla or Llama. Still, randomization in recipient selection and the short, three-round horizon limit precise coordination, so we expect a mix of clearly profitable trades (particularly involving Gorilla–Llama red–blue exchange) and a few conservative, green-mediated deals that are small but safe for everyone.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players still hold 10 of each chip. They know only their own values, so they must probe the market. Gorilla, valuing red most, will likely test a structure where they sell blue or green for red, aiming for a clearly positive surplus while not being too generous. Llama, who values blue most, will likely try to buy blue using red or green, while Whale’s first offer may be slightly prosocial and exploratory, using green or blue to elicit responses. Because no one has yet revealed preferences, acceptance thresholds are cautious but not extremely tight, and trades that give moderate gains to at least one counterparty are likely to be accepted, especially by Whale.

By the start of Round 1, accepted trades in Round 0 have already indicated patterns: Gorilla was willing to trade away blue to get red, Llama accepted a blue-for-red swap that favored Gorilla, and Whale accepted a green-for-blue deal that signaled Whale’s lower blue valuation and willingness to trade. Current holdings are skewed: Gorilla has more red and fewer blue, Llama has more blue but fewer red and green, and Whale has more green and fewer blue. Players now better infer that Gorilla likes red and Llama likes blue. Gorilla will likely push harder to consolidate red using blue/green; Llama will try to shore up green or red if cheap; Whale, having behaved cooperatively, may again propose moderate, fair-seeming deals, especially ones that move low-valued blue for higher-valued green or red.

Heading into Round 2 (the final round), the earlier trades have strengthened the revealed-preference picture: Gorilla has consistently tried to accumulate red, Llama has gravitated toward blue and accepted trades that boost blue or green at reasonable cost, and Whale has repeatedly given up blue for green or red, consistent with low blue valuation and prosocial tendencies. With only three proposal turns left, each player focuses on last-chance gains. Gorilla is now quite rich in red and relatively poor in green/blue, so any remaining offers aim to fine-tune portfolio value, but opportunities are limited. Llama wants to avoid value-destroying last moves and will only accept clearly positive trades. Whale, still relatively generous, is willing to accept small positive gains and may make final offers that are modestly favorable to partners but still profitable personally.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 of each chip color and privately know only their own payoffs, but they all see the public value ranges and total chip counts. Green chips are fixed at $0.50 for everyone, so they are safe, focal assets and a natural numeraire. Red and blue chips are where the private-value uncertainty lies: duck values both red and blue at $1.00, lion values red at $0.90 and blue at $0.50, and tiger values red at $1.00 and blue at $0.30. From an analyst perspective, red is relatively high value for all three (especially duck and tiger), blue is high only for duck, medium for lion, and low for tiger. Since players do not know each other's specific values, they must infer them from observed offers and acceptances.

Because each proposal must offer one color and request a different color, most surplus will come from trading blues and reds in ways that move chips toward the players who value them more. Duck has strong incentives to acquire both red and blue, but must be cautious about overpaying, especially given high exploitation caution. Lion is in a relatively middle position, valuing both red and blue but with a discount on blue. Tiger strongly prefers red and is willing to give up blue more easily, particularly to someone who seems to value blue more.

The random recipient rule when multiple players accept creates strategic uncertainty: proposers cannot guarantee who they will trade with. This makes simultaneous acceptance choices important, particularly for contested offers where both potential recipients gain. Given the personas, all three are exploitation-cautious and structure-seeking, so they are unlikely to accept negative-surplus or even zero-gain trades. They will also quickly use the calculator to evaluate net payoffs for each proposed exchange. Over three rounds, we expect initial conservative probes, then more targeted, value-revealing offers: duck attempts to buy blue cheaply with red or green, lion probes both sides for profitable spreads, and tiger tries to swap low-value blue for higher-value red or at least neutral green. Some potentially beneficial trades may still be declined due to misestimated counterpart values or fear of being exploited, but at least a couple of mutually positive deals are likely to execute before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the exploration phase. No one has yet revealed their valuation structure through behavior, so offers are likely to be modest and relatively symmetric, testing how others react. Duck moves first and, as a stable and exploitation-cautious reasoner, will likely probe in a way that is either slightly favorable or close to value-neutral for herself but that might look attractive to at least one counterpart. Lion, going second, is likely to test simple red–green or blue–green swaps around the common-value green anchor to see who is more eager to trade. Tiger, going third, has strong private incentives to reduce his low-value blue holdings and increase red, but does not yet know who values what, so his first proposal doubles as both a probe and an attempt to capture clear surplus.

After Round 0, one informative trade has executed: tiger successfully swapped two blue for one red with duck. This immediately reveals to both that duck values blue significantly and is willing to give up red at an attractive rate. For tiger, the trade was strongly profitable; for duck, it was slightly profitable and also a strategic investment in accumulating a chip she values highly. Lion, who did not participate in any trade, observes that duck accepted blue in exchange for red at a 2:1 rate and infers that duck's value for blue is likely high. This sets up Round 1 as a more targeted bargaining phase: duck tries to leverage blue as a high-value asset while not overpaying further for red, lion attempts to exploit or align with the emerging blue demand, and tiger seeks another opportunity to give up blue or possibly green for red if favorable terms appear.

By Round 2, duck and tiger have already completed one strongly positive-sum exchange and one modestly positive exchange between themselves, while lion has yet to secure any gains. Duck now holds more blue and red than at the start, tiger has converted much of his low-value blue into high-value red at good rates, and lion has only observational information. The structure-seeking, stability-oriented profiles make large last-minute gambles unlikely; instead, each player is looking for a final safe profit. Duck is now more cautious: her marginal value of additional red remains high, but the earlier favorable trades with tiger mean potential further gains are smaller. Lion, feeling left out and still exploitation-cautious, is unlikely to accept any proposal that isn't clearly positive. Tiger has already achieved substantial improvement and so becomes more selective as well. This tends to reduce the willingness to experiment and makes final trades harder to close, leading to conservative or declined offers unless they are obviously beneficial to the responder.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Private values differ on RED and BLUE: bear highly values BLUE ($1.00) and dislikes RED ($0.20); goose values both RED and BLUE fairly high ($0.80 each); ram values RED moderately ($0.50) and BLUE at $0.80. Because everyone sees only their own values during play, the bargaining problem is essentially a private-information trading game where each player tries to convert low-value chips (for them) into high-value chips, while inferring the others’ priorities from proposals and acceptances.

Key efficiency directions from the analyst’s perspective:
- Bear should want to dump RED and acquire BLUE.
- Goose is roughly indifferent between RED and BLUE but may slightly prefer whichever others undervalue, and GREEN is just baseline.
- Ram likes both RED (0.50) and BLUE (0.80), but RED is relatively more valuable to ram than to bear, and BLUE is more valuable to bear than to goose/ram only slightly.

Hence, mutually beneficial trades typically involve moving RED from bear to ram/goose, and possibly BLUE towards bear if priced favorably. Because acceptance is binary and simultaneous, proposers must leave the counterparty a non-negative gain. Given the players’ profiles: bear is stable and moderately prosocial but not extremely calculating; goose and ram are inequality-tolerant and relatively low on punishment, so they are willing to accept modest but positive gains without insisting on sharp splits. Limited rounds (3 per player) create some time pressure but, given their patient profiles, they are unlikely to panic; instead they will try a few sensible, slightly generous offers to ensure acceptance. Over the full game we should see a small number of clearly positive-sum trades rather than aggressive haggling, with some proposals declined when they accidentally impose a loss on all potential recipients due to unseen valuations.

In forecasting the game, we assume each proposer uses the on-screen calculator to avoid own-loss trades, often leaves a small margin for the recipient, and gradually learns rough preferences (e.g., who tends to accept RED or BLUE). Given the short horizon and their inequality-tolerant tendencies, players rarely try to exploit others with zero-gain offers and instead aim for straightforward surplus-positive deals, especially involving bear trading away under-valued RED in exchange for more valuable chips.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players are at symmetric endowments and know only their own chip values. Proposals are likely to be exploratory, testing how the others respond to swaps involving RED and BLUE. Bear, who dislikes RED and loves BLUE, will plausibly try to exchange small amounts of RED for GREEN or BLUE. Goose and ram, both more tolerant of unequal outcomes and less strict on norm enforcement, will accept any clearly profitable deal but will still reject trades that the calculator flags as losses. Because information is still thin, a couple of early proposals may be declined as players grope towards mutually beneficial exchange ratios.

Going into Round 1, the only executed trade so far moved 2 RED from bear to ram in exchange for 1 GREEN. From bear’s perspective, that trade was profitable (swapping low-value RED for higher-value GREEN); from ram’s perspective it was also profitable (getting two 0.50 REDs for one 0.50 GREEN). This reveals little about BLUE preferences but does signal that bear is willing to part with RED cheaply and that ram is willing to accumulate RED. Players now update their inferences: bear can guess ram doesn’t hate RED; ram infers bear undervalues RED. That sets up natural follow-up trades where bear continues to offload RED to ram, possibly for GREEN or BLUE, and where goose may try to position between them if she can convert GREEN into RED or BLUE for a net gain.

By Round 2, players have limited time left and have seen that some naïve ratios create losses for all potential recipients. They will tend to avoid obviously bad deals and instead craft clearly positive-sum offers. Bear has learned ram is a plausible RED-buyer; ram has seen bear’s willingness to move RED. Goose has not yet found a profitable trade and, being relatively inequality-tolerant and less guarded, is open to taking small gains. Final-round proposals are therefore likely to include slightly generous RED-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-GREEN swaps that tip both sides into the black, with at least one more deal executing before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game centers on trading privately valued red and blue chips against common-value green chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so any trade involving green is transparent in dollar terms to all parties. The strategic uncertainty comes from red and blue chips, whose values differ by player but are privately known.

Valuations:
- lion-blue-472 (Lion): BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.50
- octopus-green-4361 (Octopus): BLUE=$0.10, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.20
- ram-purple-2824 (Ram): BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.30

In value terms, Ram values blue the most and red the least; Octopus values both red and blue quite low relative to green; Lion values red and green equally and blue slightly more. Purely from final payoffs, there is scope for Pareto-improving trades moving blue from Octopus/Lion to Ram in exchange for green, and moving some red from Ram/Octopus toward Lion (who values it more than Ram, and significantly more than Octopus) in exchange for green or blue. However, players do not know others' exact valuations. They only see their own surplus impact for any given trade via a calculator, and responders see their own gain/loss once a proposal appears.

Because acceptance decisions are based on each player's private calculator, proposals that are only weakly favorable or that move chips away from green (the safe, salient common-value asset) may be declined, especially early. Also, if both non-senders accept, the recipient is chosen randomly, so proposers must design offers that are acceptable to at least one potential partner but robust to possibly trading with either. Personas matter: Lion tends to be prosocial but careful and numerate; Octopus is cooperative, inequality-tolerant, and not strongly punitive; Ram is resource-protective and willing to exploit gains when clear.

Over three rounds (nine total turns), players will explore simple, easy-to-understand trades that visibly create value for themselves while appearing plausible to at least one counterparty. Early turns likely feature conservative, near-fair trades involving small quantities (often with green) as players test the market and others' willingness to accept. As the game proceeds and remaining opportunities shrink, Ram is especially motivated to acquire blue cheaply from Octopus and possibly from Lion, while both Octopus and Lion are willing to move low-value chips (for them) in exchange for green or, occasionally, slightly higher-valued colors. Nevertheless, risk of rejection and lack of direct communication limit how close the final allocation comes to the fully efficient reallocation suggested by the private values.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, all players still hold 10 of each chip type, and no one has observed any trading behavior yet. Uncertainty about others' valuations is highest. Given their personas, Lion is likely to start with a relatively fair, small trade that uses green as the payment currency, since its value is common knowledge. Octopus will experiment with improving his position in green relative to red/blue without appearing too exploitative. Ram, being resource-protective and valuing blue highly, will likely test the waters by trying to acquire blue at a price that is clearly profitable but still potentially acceptable to at least one counterpart. Most trades will be sized conservatively to avoid big visible losses to responders.

After round 0, some structure has emerged. Lion declined a slightly value-losing first offer, then accepted Octopus's favorable green-for-red trade, signaling she is willing to trade when her calculator shows a decent gain. Ram succeeded in acquiring blue from Lion in exchange for green, revealing that Lion is open to swapping green for higher-valued chips when the numbers work. Octopus has improved his green holdings at the cost of low-value red, while Ram has converted green into very high-value blue. In this round, Lion is now somewhat chip-imbalanced (fewer green, more red and blue), Octopus sits heavy in green and low in red, and Ram holds strong blue but a bit less green. Players will refine earlier patterns: Octopus will likely look to dump more low-value red for green or occasionally blue, Lion will try to rebuild green or adjust her red/blue mix, and Ram will continue trying to cheaply accumulate blue, though he may face resistance if his partners infer his strong preference.

Entering the final round, there is a clearer picture of tendencies. Lion has shown she rejects even small personal losses and accepts moderate gains, and that she is reluctant to part with green when it does not obviously improve her mix. Octopus has repeatedly accepted trades where he gives up low-value red or gains from Ram's undervalued red, and he declined unfavorable green-for-blue terms, revealing some learning about blue's potential value. Ram has aggressively pursued blue and successfully used both green and red to buy it, while declining to overpay with green. With only three turns left, each player faces a last opportunity to tweak their portfolio. Lion, somewhat risk-averse and prosocial, will propose a modest, almost break-even trade that she expects might appeal to a partner but will not insist on forcing a big surplus. Octopus, sitting on surplus green and extra red, will try one more attempt to improve his bundle, possibly by moving red to someone who values it more. Ram, still most hungry for blue, will frame a final offer that is clearly profitable to him but not so lopsided that it is automatically rejected, aiming at Octopus, who has proved more willing to trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading privately valued red and blue chips plus a common-value green chip. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Greens are always worth $0.50, so there is no disagreement about their value. The gains from trade come from the differences in red/blue valuations: buffalo highly values RED ($0.90) and dislikes BLUE ($0.10); duck highly values BLUE ($0.90) and is relatively indifferent to RED ($0.40); cat modestly prefers BLUE ($0.20) over RED ($0.40) but is much less extreme than buffalo and duck. That structure creates strong, mutual incentives for buffalo to ship BLUE to duck in exchange for RED, and for both of them to protect their own high-value color. Green chips, being common-value, tend to be used only sparingly as a side payment or to signal cooperativeness, because giving one up is a certain loss to both sides. Uncertainty arises because each player only observes their own private valuations. They know that others could value chips differently, but not how strongly, and they must infer these from observed offers and acceptances across rounds. Both cat and duck are relatively inequality-tolerant and not strong norm enforcers, so they are more likely to accept small but positive surplus trades, especially early. Buffalo is cooperative and socially adaptive but dislikes uncertainty and losses, so she is likely to propose simple, self-beneficial exchanges that avoid red calculator numbers. The random allocation of the recipient when multiple players accept also injects strategic uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner if both are willing to accept, making it risky to design extreme offers that are good with one partner and bad with the other. Over three rounds, players are expected to experiment early with plausible cross-color exchanges to map out others’ implied values, and then converge (if possible) on mutually beneficial red–blue trades, while largely avoiding giving up green unless the calculator shows a clear net monetary gain.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip and have no information about others’ private values. The strategic priority is exploration with low-risk offers that clearly benefit the proposer according to the calculator. Buffalo is likely to test a red-for-blue trade that converts her low-value BLUE into high-value RED. Cat and duck will respond based on their own calculators: duck, who values BLUE highly, will be cautious about giving it away; cat’s moderate valuations make her more flexible but still sensitive to losing too much value. Because any trade now determines subsequent holdings, accepted deals can quickly create asymmetries that shape bargaining power in later rounds.

Entering Round 1, the only executed trade is buffalo’s initial BLUE-for-RED deal with cat. Buffalo now has more RED and fewer BLUE; cat has extra BLUE and fewer RED; duck’s holdings remain symmetric. This trade already reveals that cat was willing to give up some RED for BLUE, suggesting she doesn’t value RED dramatically more than BLUE. Buffalo infers that BLUE has at least modest value to cat. Both buffalo and duck will now be cautious trading away their high-value color (RED for buffalo, BLUE for duck) without clear compensation. Players also update beliefs that green seems ‘expensive’ because no one traded it in Round 0. As a result, proposals may tilt toward pure red–blue exchanges, with more conservative quantities as they refine their sense of others’ thresholds.

By Round 2, buffalo and duck have engaged in mutually beneficial red–blue swaps and now have clearer beliefs that they are mirror-image valuers: buffalo values RED far more than BLUE, and duck the reverse. Cat has made only one accepted trade and has observed both others decline green trades, indicating that green is treated as a strict common-value asset. With only one round of offers left, players shift from exploration to extracting final gains. Buffalo, holding relatively more RED after trades with both cat and duck, still wants to offload some BLUE for RED if possible, but must avoid overpaying now that others are more informed. Duck may propose a last BLUE-for-RED exchange that leaves him even more BLUE-heavy, and cat might attempt a small, safe trade or may passively decline disadvantageous offers, protecting her remaining mixed portfolio. Given the timing and better information, accepted trades now tend to be smaller and closer to zero-sum, with fewer speculative experiments.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each participant starts with 10 of each chip, so initial endowments are symmetric but valuations differ substantially. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so they function as a natural "currency" and safe store of value. Red and blue chips, by contrast, have sharply divergent values: butterfly values RED=$0.30 and BLUE=$0.10, kangaroo values RED=$0.70 and BLUE=$0.20, and squid values RED=$0.40 and BLUE=$0.80. This creates large potential gains from trade, especially when low-value chips for one player are high-value chips for another (e.g., BLUE is low for butterfly, medium for kangaroo, and very high for squid). The UI surplus calculator lets each person instantly see their own gain or loss for any proposal, so inefficient or exploitative offers are likely to be rejected unless they leave the responder with a clear positive surplus. Given the alternating-proposer structure and three rounds, players must balance patience (waiting for better offers) against the risk that no mutually beneficial trade will occur later. Personality priors suggest butterfly and squid are more cooperative and structured, likely to look for mutually beneficial, clearly positive-sum trades. Kangaroo is more self-protective, with high exploitation caution and lower generosity, so she will typically demand a good surplus margin and avoid trades that are too close to break-even. Uncertainty arises because each player does not know the others’ private red/blue values, so early in the game they must infer how valuable each chip color is to others from which offers get accepted or declined. Over the three rounds, a plausible dynamic is exploratory but cautious proposals in early turns (testing how attractive green or blue is to others), followed by more targeted and efficient trades as players update beliefs about who values which chip. Because acceptances are simultaneous and the partner is randomly selected when there are multiple acceptances, proposers may also structure offers in ways that are attractive to only one likely counterpart; this both reduces competition for the offer and improves alignment of gains. Altogether, one would expect at least a few accepted trades that move red chips toward kangaroo and blue chips toward squid, often mediated by green as the safe medium, while butterfly opportunistically trades away her lowest-value colors when the surplus is clear and risk is low.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, all players hold 10 of each chip and know only their own valuations. Butterfly and squid are generally cooperative and thoughtful; kangaroo is guarded and surplus-seeking. No one yet knows that kangaroo values red highly and squid values blue highly, though that is where the largest gains from trade lie. Round 0 is therefore likely to involve relatively simple, low-risk exploratory offers using green as a reference, as players probe how responsive others are to small but clearly positive deals. Responders will be quite sensitive to their on-screen surplus calculators, declining any offer that shows a loss or a trivial gain relative to the effort and risk, especially given there are multiple future turns.

After round 0, holdings have shifted modestly due to butterfly’s accepted trade with kangaroo: butterfly has slightly more green and fewer blue; kangaroo has more blue and less green; squid remains at the initial endowment. Observationally, everyone now knows that kangaroo is willing to trade away green for blue at a modest but positive cost, and that butterfly is happy to offload blue. This hints that blue is at least somewhat valuable to kangaroo and relatively cheap to butterfly. The two declined offers in round 0 tell everyone that neither butterfly nor squid is willing to give up red for green on kangaroo’s initial terms, and that neither butterfly nor kangaroo found squid’s green-for-blue exchange attractive. Players now have slightly sharper beliefs: kangaroo likely values red a lot (given her attempt to buy it), squid likely values blue quite a bit (given her initial buy attempt), and butterfly appears to view blue as her weakest color. Round 1 should therefore feature more targeted trades: butterfly may again monetize low-value blue into green, kangaroo may refine her attempt to buy red with terms that are less one-sided, and squid may adjust her buy prices for blue to make them attractive enough given what others have just rejected.

Going into the final round, a clear pattern has emerged: butterfly reliably trades away her least-valued color (blue) and is willing to sell red when the surplus is decent; kangaroo has been steadily trying to accumulate red while occasionally offloading blue or green; squid has been actively trying to buy blue and is willing to pay in either green or, if the surplus is strong enough, red. By now, observed acceptances and rejections give a fairly sharp common belief: (1) kangaroo values red highly and is cautious but will accept good surplus moves even that reduce her red slightly if compensated with green; (2) squid values blue very highly and views red as moderately valuable, so she avoids bad red-for-blue ratios; (3) butterfly is the most flexible, trading based on clear positive net gains and showing less concern for unequal outcomes. With only one proposal turn left for each player, the incentives are to capture any remaining obvious gains from trade, even if somewhat unbalanced, because there will be no future opportunities or punishment. Proposers should offer deals that yield a clear positive surplus to one specific target player, incorporating observed acceptance thresholds. Marginal improvements—like trading low-value blue from butterfly or kangaroo to squid, or moving a bit more red toward kangaroo—are likely, provided the offering side does not incur a visible loss on their calculator.
