parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-chip-type bargaining game with incomplete information about others’ values but complete information about one’s own payoffs from any proposed trade. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 each. The private asymmetry is in red and blue: Dog and Turtle both value blue at $1 and red at $0.50; Parrot values red higher ($0.60) and blue much lower ($0.30). Efficiently, Dog/Turtle should try to buy blue from Parrot (since it’s cheap for Parrot) and sell red to Parrot (since Parrot values it more), with green chips serving as a neutral medium for compromise. However, none of them sees the others’ exact values; they must infer them gradually from which trades are proposed and accepted. The UI calculator means nobody will knowingly accept a trade that reduces their own payoff, so all executed trades must be weakly beneficial for both sides ex ante. Dog and Parrot are relatively empathic and inequality-tolerant, so they are willing to leave some surplus for partners and explore mutually beneficial trades. Turtle is more guarded against exploitation and will only accept clearly positive, simple offers. Over three rounds, Dog is likely to experiment early with green-for-red/blue to feel out acceptability, Parrot to search for red gains while offloading less-valued blue, and Turtle to wait for clean, obviously favorable terms. Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection, proposers must balance generosity (to entice more than one acceptor) with the risk that the ‘wrong’ partner is randomly chosen. Given the short horizon and the personas, the game is likely to see modest surplus-creating trades, mostly shifting blue from Parrot to Dog/Turtle and some red towards Parrot, with a few exploratory or too-imbalanced proposals declined in early turns as everyone tests where the gains-from-trade boundaries lie.

rounds.round_explanation: All players start with 10 of each color and no history. Dog and Turtle both privately see blue as very valuable and red as weak; Parrot sees red as moderately best and blue as mediocre. In this first round, they are mainly probing. Dog, who is adaptive and inequality-tolerant, is likely to start with a small, safe-looking exploratory offer involving green as a neutral medium. Parrot, prosocial and structured, will probably propose something that modestly improves their red holdings without looking too aggressive, while still learning how others react. Turtle, more guarded and low in cognitive reflection, will prefer simple, obviously beneficial trades and avoid anything that could be exploitative or confusing. Acceptances this round are driven by clear, small positive gains and easy-to-interpret proposals.

After round 0, Parrot and Turtle have executed a trade: Turtle has slightly more blue and less red; Parrot has swapped some low-value blue for somewhat higher-value red, which is good for both. Dog saw that Parrot was willing to trade blue for red and that Turtle values blue enough to pay red, but doesn’t know exact valuations. Turtle sees that Parrot is willing to give up blue for red, reinforcing that blue might be more valuable to Turtle than to Parrot. In round 1, Dog is likely to target Parrot’s blue explicitly, offering red that is cheap for Dog but potentially attractive to Parrot. Parrot, noticing that both others seem interested in blue, may use green or red to buy blue-advantageously. Turtle continues to be selective, but now has a bit more confidence that red-for-blue trades can be good.

Going into the final round, some of the efficient reallocation pattern has emerged: Parrot has already offloaded several low-value blue chips to Dog and Turtle in exchange for red and a bit of green, which all sides found individually profitable. Dog and Turtle now both hold more blue than at the start, in line with their higher valuations, while Parrot has more red relative to blue. Everyone has seen which chip directions tend to clear, so proposals now are more targeted and somewhat larger, but constrained by the short horizon and risk aversion, especially for Turtle. Dog will try one more moderate blue-grab from Parrot using red; Parrot may test a small red-for-blue swing to Turtle to fine-tune holdings; Turtle will propose only if the trade is obviously positive and simple, otherwise preferring to lock in existing gains. Because this is the last chance, players are slightly more willing to make generous offers that yield small but sure gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 for everyone) and two private-value colors (RED and BLUE). Here, RED is more valuable to unicorn-blue-7096 ($0.50) than to badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 ($0.30 each), while BLUE is low-value and symmetric ($0.10 for all). Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Because GREEN is fixed at $0.50 and starts equally held, it acts as a kind of neutral currency; BLUE is a low-value surplus token; and RED is the main source of potential gains-from-trade, especially in trades that move RED from badger/peacock to unicorn in exchange for GREEN or, secondarily, BLUE. Rationally, each player’s surplus calculator means they will rarely accept a trade that lowers their own monetary payoff, and proposers can easily tune offers to be strictly profitable for themselves. Yet players know only their own chip values, so they do not know that unicorn values RED more—this creates strategic uncertainty: unicorn is willing to pay more for RED than badger or peacock expect, and unicorn cannot be sure whether others see RED similarly. Given the personas, peacock is analytical and cooperative and is likely to seek mutually beneficial trades, avoid obvious exploitation, and adjust offer ratios as he learns others’ willingness to trade. Badger is structure-seeking and patient but somewhat low on behavioral stability, likely to start with cautious, symmetric or “fair-looking” offers that protect against loss and gradually adjust once she sees accept/reject feedback. Unicorn is cooperative and inequality-tolerant with weak norm-enforcement, so he is relatively willing to accept small positive gains and may agree to trades that create substantial advantage for the counterpart if they are clearly non-loss-making for him. Across the three rounds, the main dynamic tension is between exploiting unicorn’s higher RED valuation and maintaining relationships; the limited horizon (only nine total proposal turns) pushes players toward straightforward, low-risk trades once mutually profitable exchange patterns are discovered. Public history of executed trades progressively reveals who values what, so later rounds are more targeted: unicorn increasingly bids for RED, while badger and peacock try to convert excess RED into GREEN or at least shed low-value BLUE, all while reacting to the risk that any attractive offer might be snapped up by either counterpart due to random recipient selection when multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and have no information about others’ valuations beyond the public ranges. They will likely begin with simple, low-stakes trades that look roughly symmetric in quantities and use GREEN as a benchmark since its value is known and focal. Badger, who prefers structure and is moderately cooperative, is inclined to propose something that appears fair and safe, probably involving exchanging a bit of her relatively less-valued RED for GREEN. Peacock, being highly analytical and cooperative, is likely to read the first proposal’s responses as signals and then craft a more clearly mutually beneficial trade, potentially leveraging BLUE as cheap currency. Unicorn, cooperative and tolerant of unequal outcomes, will likely probe whether others are willing to trade away RED, but initially without revealing extreme ratios, to avoid appearing exploitative. Accept/reject decisions in this round are mostly driven by immediate surplus (from the calculator) and simple fairness heuristics rather than fine-tuned beliefs about others’ values.

By the start of round 1, all players have observed that unicorn was willing to accept 1 RED for 1 GREEN (signaling RED is not obviously low-value to him) and pay 5 BLUE for 2 RED, and that both badger and peacock are open to giving up some GREEN for BLUE when compensated. Badger has slightly increased GREEN and given up some RED and BLUE; peacock has traded BLUE for GREEN; unicorn has converted BLUE into RED and given up some GREEN. This pattern suggests to attentive players that BLUE is low-value to everyone and that unicorn may particularly value RED. Badger, liking structure and having seen unicorn pay a premium in BLUE for RED, may now design offers that offload her surplus BLUE in exchange for GREEN or RED from unicorn. Peacock, analytically minded, updates that BLUE is good trade bait and that unicorn is a natural RED buyer, and will try to earn modest but certain gains, keeping offers that are good for both sides. Unicorn, aware that others are willing to take BLUE and part with some RED or GREEN, continues to accumulate RED using BLUE, but may temper ratios slightly to keep offers attractive enough to be accepted consistently.

Entering round 2, the final round, players see a clear pattern: BLUE has repeatedly been traded away by unicorn in exchange for RED and occasionally by badger and peacock for GREEN or RED; GREEN is treated as a stable, valuable anchor; and unicorn is consistently willing to trade substantial BLUE for RED. Badger has ended up with more GREEN and still some RED, having sold off BLUE; peacock has strengthened his GREEN and RED holdings while also offloading some BLUE; unicorn has amassed extra RED but significantly depleted BLUE. With only three proposal turns left, there is little incentive for subtle signaling; each proposer focuses on immediate, calculator-confirmed gains that are likely to be accepted. Badger, satisfied with her GREEN position, may try one more BLUE-for-GREEN or modest BLUE-for-RED trade. Peacock, valuing fairness and analytical balance, aims for one last mutually beneficial but not extreme trade—perhaps selling BLUE or buying a bit more RED if unicorn still has surplus BLUE or RED imbalances. Unicorn, in his final proposal, tries to convert any remaining BLUE into RED at a favorable, but still win-win, ratio, expecting that badger and peacock will accept small but positive gains even if they suspect unicorn benefits more.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. The private differences are in RED and BLUE: dog values BLUE the most ($1.00) and RED at $0.80; mouse values BLUE at $0.60 and RED very low at $0.20; owl values BLUE highly at $1.00 and RED at $0.30. From a surplus standpoint, BLUE is always relatively valuable (especially for dog and owl), while RED is generally low value for mouse and owl but still moderately valuable for dog. GREEN sits in the middle at $0.50 for everyone. This implies efficient trades will typically move BLUE toward dog and owl and move RED away from mouse (and somewhat away from owl) toward dog, with GREEN often used as a balancing medium. However, each player knows only their own payoffs, and they cannot see the others’ chip values, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. The personas suggest dog and mouse are generosity-forward and cooperation-oriented, while owl is more analytical and socially adaptive with moderate exploitation caution. Players also know that if multiple partners accept a proposal, the recipient is chosen randomly, so a proposer cannot tailor a deal to a specific partner once it is on the table. The on-screen surplus calculators mean no one will knowingly accept a net-loss trade for themselves. Across three rounds of three proposals each, we should see small, individually profitable trades that gradually reveal that mouse is willing to shed RED, dog is quite willing to send away some GREEN or RED to obtain BLUE, and owl likes BLUE but is cautious about overpaying with GREEN. Uncertainty stays high because there are only a few turns, but the prosocial tendencies of dog and mouse make mutually beneficial trades likely when they are easy to identify from each player’s own perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one yet knows the others’ valuations, so the first few offers are likely to be exploratory but still slightly favorable from the proposer’s perspective (shown by their surplus calculator). Dog, with very high prosociality and low exploitation caution, is likely to propose a modest, nearly fair-looking trade that still gains a bit of BLUE or GREEN value. Mouse will be prepared to trade away some of their low-value RED if they can get BLUE or even GREEN in return at no loss. Owl will think more strategically, accepting only clear gains and starting to infer others’ priorities from what they ask for and offer.

By the start of Round 1, the group has seen that mouse is willing to give up multiple RED for BLUE and that owl was eager to buy BLUE and to trade away RED for GREEN. This suggests to both dog and mouse that owl has relatively low value for RED and good value for BLUE and GREEN, and suggests to owl that mouse places low value on RED and at least moderate value on GREEN. Dog now has fewer GREEN chips from the first declined offer attempt and has observed that neither partner was willing to pay what she asked for BLUE. She will likely adjust to offer more generous surplus to potential partners when trying to acquire BLUE. Mouse, having profited nicely by selling RED and then gaining GREEN, may continue to dump RED if he can do so for even small gains. Owl, more informed now, will shape offers that trade away RED or possibly some GREEN in order to obtain high-value BLUE, but must be careful not to propose terms that are so favorable to himself that no one accepts.

Going into the final round, everyone has some evidence about others’ priorities: mouse repeatedly tries to offload RED; dog shows willingness to part with RED when the price in BLUE is right; owl has traded away both RED and some GREEN but has also bought BLUE. Each player now knows the game ends after this round, so there is no reason to hold back chips for future bargaining. The best strategy is to look for any remaining positive-surplus exchanges given their own values. Dog, still cooperation-oriented and dislike of uncertainty, may propose a modest trade that is almost break-even for her but seemingly attractive to at least one partner. Mouse will again try to turn low-value RED into either BLUE or GREEN, but after several trades, there may be less appetite from others if positions have shifted. Owl will aim to fine-tune his portfolio, seeking last small gains in BLUE if possible, while avoiding any losses. Some offers will now be declined because marginal gains are thin, but at least one more mutually beneficial trade is likely as players try to convert their weakest chips into stronger ones.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red and blue chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color, but their dollar values differ by person: alligator values red highest (0.60) relative to blue (0.50) and green (0.50); buffalo is fairly balanced with mild preference for red (0.50) over blue (0.20) but equal to green (0.50); rabbit strongly prefers red (1.00) over green (0.50) and blue (0.30). This structure creates clear gains from trade: in particular, rabbit and alligator both value red more than buffalo does, while buffalo cares relatively more about turning low-value blue into green/red. Green is common-value at 0.50 for everyone, so trading red or blue for green makes sense when it allows a player to move chips away from their lowest-valued color. However, each player only observes their own values; they see the public range for reds and blues (0.10–1.00), so they know others may value some colors very differently.

Given their personas, alligator is a logical, inequality-tolerant reasoner focused on maximizing own payoff and not strongly enforcing fairness, so we expect calculated but somewhat self-focused proposals that secure at least small positive surplus. Buffalo is cooperation-oriented and generosity-forward, often willing to accept mutually beneficial or even slightly disadvantageous trades to keep things moving and avoid uncertainty. Rabbit is prosocial and reflective, but also inequality-tolerant and less guarded, so she tends to accept favorable or mildly unbalanced offers if they help others and still leave her better off. Because responders see their exact net gain/loss in real time, blatantly negative-sum offers for them are almost always rejected; the bargaining happens over positive-sum, roughly fair-seeming splits where one side might gain a bit more.

The main strategic tension comes from asymmetric information about valuations and the random resolution when multiple players accept. Proposers cannot target a specific responder; if both non-senders would gain and accept, the realized partner is chosen randomly. That encourages proposers to frame trades that are reasonably attractive to both potential partners, or where they are indifferent between which partner they actually trade with. Over the three rounds, each player has three proposal opportunities. Because no leftover chips carry to other games and there is no explicit penalty for failed trades, people often become more flexible over time, especially the more cooperative players.

We therefore anticipate that early moves will involve cautious, simple, one-color-for-one-color swaps that offer both sides a small positive surplus. As players observe who accepts what, they adjust expectations of others’ likely valuations. Alligator’s reasoning ability and low norm-enforcement drive him toward clean arbitrage-like trades that exploit likely undervaluation of some colors by others. Buffalo’s cooperation orientation makes her a frequent accepter of modest-gain trades, especially when she can convert low-value blue into higher-value red or green. Rabbit’s strong red preference means she is willing to give up some green and blue to build up red holdings, accepting deals that look slightly tilted but clearly beneficial. The forecast below describes one plausible full playthrough, with modest but consistent gains for all three as they gradually discover and exploit value differences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip type. No one has any information yet about others’ private valuations beyond the common ranges. Given their personas, we expect conservative, clearly positive-sum proposals that test the waters. Alligator, as a strong reasoner with weak fairness enforcement, is likely to seek a small but safe gain by thinning out his relatively least-preferred color (blue) or exchanging moderate amounts of red for green if he believes others may undervalue green. Buffalo and rabbit, both cooperative and relatively accepting, will tend to approve trades that visibly increase their own surplus, especially if they align with their preferred colors (buffalo away from blue, rabbit toward red). The randomness in partner selection when both accept makes it sensible for proposers to offer terms that look at least modestly good to both potential recipients.

After round 0, the public history shows that both buffalo and rabbit were willing to trade in ways that reveal some structure: buffalo repeatedly moved away from blue toward green and red, and rabbit clearly signaled a willingness to give up green for red. Alligator, who initiated a green-for-blue trade, should infer that buffalo may value green at least as much as he does and value blue very little, while rabbit’s acceptance of a blue-for-red trade indicates that she values red significantly more than blue. This narrows the plausible valuation sets in alligator’s and buffalo’s minds, even without exact numbers. Prosocial tendencies and successful trades in round 0 build trust, so players become more willing to propose and accept somewhat larger, still obviously positive-sum exchanges. In round 1 we expect more targeted attempts: alligator trying to get additional red from buffalo at a decent rate, buffalo continuing to offload low-value blue, and rabbit aggressively building her red pile even if it means giving up more green or blue.

By round 2, the trading pattern is clear: buffalo repeatedly gives up blue and sometimes red in exchange for green or modest red gains; rabbit gladly swaps green and blue for red; alligator uses careful exchanges to tilt his portfolio slightly toward higher-valued red while not overpaying for it. Each player has observed several accepted trades, which reinforces their beliefs: everyone now strongly suspects that buffalo dislikes blue, that rabbit loves red, and that alligator is a measured trader who will only propose offers with visible gains for him. With only one round left and no future negotiations to preserve, players are more willing to execute any remaining positive-sum opportunities, even if they slightly favor one side. Proposals therefore concentrate on exploiting the clearest comparative advantages: rabbit tries one more time to acquire red from buffalo; alligator closes with a moderate value-improving swap involving his remaining less-preferred color; buffalo, still prosocial, offers a final mutually beneficial trade shifting her away from blue.
